title.zh: 卷五十三 周黃徐姜申屠列傳
title.en: Volume 53: Biographies of Zhou, Huang, Xu, Jiang, Shentu
title.raw: 卷五十三 周黃徐姜申屠列傳 Volume 53: Biographies of Zhou, Huang, Xu, Jiang, Shentu

sentences.zh: 後漢書卷五十三 周黃徐姜申屠列傳 第四十三

translations.literal: Book of Later Han, Scroll 53: Collective Biographies of Zhou, Huang, Xu, Jiang, and Shentu, Number 43.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Later Han, scroll 53: biographies of Zhou, Huang, Xu, Jiang, and Shentu, the forty-third set.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「君子之道，或出或處，或默或語。

translations.literal: The Changes says: "The way of the gentleman is sometimes to go forth, sometimes to stay in retirement; sometimes to be silent, sometimes to speak."
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes says: "The path of the gentleman may lead him out into office or back into seclusion; he may hold his tongue or speak aloud."

sentences.zh: 」[一]孔子稱「蘧伯玉邦有道則仕，邦無道則可卷而懷也」。

translations.literal: " [One] Confucius praised [saying]: "When Qu Boyu's state has the Way, then he serves in office; when it lacks the Way, then he can roll up [his principles] and keep them in his bosom.""
translations.idiomatic: " Confucius once said of Qu Boyu: "When the state is well ordered he takes office; when it is not, he can roll up his learning and tuck it away in his heart.""

sentences.zh: [二]然用捨之端，君子之所以存其誠也。

translations.literal: [Two] Yet the beginnings of employment and rejection are how the gentleman preserves his sincerity.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Still, the whole question of whether one is employed or set aside is precisely where the gentleman proves his inner truth.

sentences.zh: [三]故其行也，則濡足蒙垢，出身以□時；

translations.literal: [Three] Therefore in his going forth, then he wets his feet and bears filth, emerging from himself in order to [lacuna] the age;
translations.idiomatic: [Three] So when he steps forward, he is willing to muddy his feet and bear disgrace, leaving private life to devote himself to the needs of the times.

sentences.zh: [四]及其止也，則窮棲茹菽，臧寶以迷國。

translations.literal: [Four] As for his stopping, then in utter destitution he dwells and chews beans, hiding his treasure thereby to mislead the state.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] When he holds back, he may live in the humblest poverty on coarse fare, concealing his gifts and leaving the realm none the wiser.

sentences.zh: [五] 注[一]上系之詞也。

translations.literal: [Five] Commentary [one]: It is a word that ties on from above.
translations.idiomatic: [Five] Note one: This is a connective phrase linking to what precedes.

sentences.zh: 言賢哲所行，其趣異也。

translations.literal: It means that in what the worthy and wise do, their inclinations differ.
translations.idiomatic: It is saying that wise men may walk different paths according to their temperaments.

sentences.zh: 注[二]論論蘧伯玉名瑗，□大夫也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Lun discusses Qu Boyu, whose name was Huan, [lacuna] grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Analects treats Qu Boyu, styled Huan, who served as a minister of Wei.

sentences.zh: 卷而懷謂不預時政，不忤於人者也。

translations.literal: "Roll up and keep in the bosom" means not participating in the government of the time, and not giving offense to others.
translations.idiomatic: To "roll it up and put it away" is to stay clear of current politics and give no one cause for resentment.

sentences.zh: 注[三]誠，實也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Sincerity means substance.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: Here "sincerity" means genuineness, solid reality.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：「用之則行，捨之則臧。

translations.literal: Confucius said: "When they employ him, then he acts; when they set him aside, then he hides."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said: "When the ruler calls on him, he goes into action; when he is passed over, he withdraws and keeps his virtue hidden."

sentences.zh: 」易曰：「閒邪存其誠。」

translations.literal: " The Changes says: "Guard against evil and preserve his sincerity.""
translations.idiomatic: " The Book of Changes says: "Shut the door on wrong and hold fast to what is true in you.""

sentences.zh: 注[四]新序曰：「申徒狄非時，將自投河，崔嘉聞而止之曰：『吾聞聖人從事於天地之閒，人之父母也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The New Arrangements says: "Shentu Di deemed the times wrong and was about to throw himself into the river; Cui Jia heard of it and stopped him, saying: 'I have heard that the sage conducts his business between Heaven and Earth; he is the father and mother of the people.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: Liu Xiang's New Arrangements records: "Shentu Di despaired of the age and meant to drown himself in the river. Cui Jia heard of it and checked him, saying, 'I have heard that the sage moves between heaven and earth as a parent to humankind.'"

sentences.zh: 今為濡足之故，不救溺人乎？

translations.literal: Now for the sake of wetting your feet, will you not save the drowning man?"
translations.idiomatic: Would you really let a few wet feet keep you from pulling someone who is drowning?"

sentences.zh: 』」注[五]爾雅曰：「啜，茹也。

translations.literal: '"" Commentary [five]: The Erya says: "Chuo means to chew."
translations.idiomatic: '"" Note five: The Erya glosses chuo as "to chew, to eat."

sentences.zh: 」孫卿子曰：「君子啜菽飲水，非愚也，是節然也。」

translations.literal: " Master Sun Qing said: "The gentleman chews beans and drinks water; it is not stupidity, but such is restraint.""
translations.idiomatic: " Xunzi said: "The gentleman may live on beans and water alone; that is not folly but the shape his moderation takes.""

sentences.zh: 論語曰，陽貨謂孔子曰：「懷其寶而迷其邦，可謂仁乎？」

translations.literal: The Analects says: Yang Huo said to Confucius: "Hiding his treasure and misleading his state—can this be called benevolence?"
translations.idiomatic: The Analects relates how Yang Huo challenged Confucius: "To hug your talents to yourself while your country goes astray—can you call that humanity?"

sentences.zh: 太原閔仲叔者，[一]世稱節士，雖周黨之潔清，自以弗及也。

translations.literal: There was Min Zhongshu of Taiyuan; [one] the age called him a man of integrity; although Zhou Dang's purity and cleanness, he himself thought he did not reach him.
translations.idiomatic: Min Zhongshu of Taiyuan was renowned in his day as a man of unbending principle; even Zhou Dang, famous for spotless conduct, admitted he could not match him.

sentences.zh: 黨見其含菽飲水，遺以生蒜，受而不食。

translations.literal: Dang saw that he held beans in his mouth and drank water, and presented him with raw garlic; he received it but did not eat it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Dang saw him subsisting on beans and water alone and sent him fresh garlic as a gift; Min accepted it but never touched the food.

sentences.zh: [二]建武中，應司徒侯霸之辟，既至，霸不及政事，徒勞苦而已。

translations.literal: [Two] In the Jianwu period, he answered the summons of Minister of Education Hou Ba; when he had arrived, Ba did not reach to affairs of government, but only toiled and distressed him, that was all.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Under Emperor Guangwu he accepted a call from Minister over the Masses Hou Ba, but once he arrived Hou never spoke of state business and only put him through empty formalities.

sentences.zh: [三]仲叔恨曰：「始蒙嘉命，且喜且懼；

translations.literal: [Three] Zhongshu resentfully said: "At first receiving your fine command, I was both pleased and fearful;
translations.idiomatic: [Three] Min said bitterly: "When I first received your gracious appointment I was both honored and anxious."

sentences.zh: 今見明公，喜懼皆去。

translations.literal: Now having seen the enlightened lord, pleasure and fear have both gone.
translations.idiomatic: Now that I have met Your Lordship, neither feeling remains.

sentences.zh: 以仲叔為不足問邪，不當辟也。

translations.literal: If you take Zhongshu as not worth asking, then you ought not to have summoned him.
translations.idiomatic: If you thought me unworthy of consultation, you should never have called me forth.

sentences.zh: 辟而不問，是失人也。

translations.literal: To summon and not to ask is to lose men.
translations.idiomatic: To invite a man and then ignore him is to betray the very purpose of recruitment.

sentences.zh: 」遂辭出，投劾而去。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he declined and went out, cast down his accusation document and departed.
translations.idiomatic: " With that he took his leave, filed a resignation of his appointment, and walked away.

sentences.zh: [四] 復以博士征，不至。

translations.literal: [Four] Again they summoned him as Erudite; he did not come.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] The court summoned him again as a court academician; he refused to appear.

sentences.zh: 客居安邑。

translations.literal: As a guest he dwelt in Anyi.
translations.idiomatic: He lived on in Anyi as a resident guest.

sentences.zh: 老病家貧，不能得肉，日買質肝一片，屠者或不肯與，安邑令聞，□吏常給焉。

translations.literal: Old and ill, his household was poor and he could not obtain meat; each day he bought one slice of pig liver on credit, and the butcher sometimes was unwilling to give it; the magistrate of Anyi heard and ordered the clerk constantly to supply him.
translations.idiomatic: Age and illness found him in desperate poverty: he could afford no meat and bought a single slice of liver each day on credit, which butchers sometimes refused. When the magistrate of Anyi learned of it, he ordered an underling to provide it regularly.

sentences.zh: 仲叔怪而問之，知，乃歎曰：「閔仲叔豈以口腹累安邑邪？

translations.literal: Zhongshu wondered and asked about it; when he knew, then he sighed, saying: "Can Min Zhongshu by mouth and belly burden Anyi?"
translations.idiomatic: Min wondered at this, asked how it had come about, and when he learned the truth he sighed: "Surely Min Zhongshu must not let his stomach become a burden on Anyi?"

sentences.zh: 」遂去，客沛。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he left and guested in Pei.
translations.idiomatic: " He left at once and moved on to lodge in Pei.

sentences.zh: 以壽終。

translations.literal: He ended by longevity.
translations.idiomatic: He died a natural death in old age.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝沉書曰：「閔貢字仲叔。」

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xie Chen's book says: "Min Gong, courtesy name Zhongshu."
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Xie Chen's history gives his name as Min Gong, styled Zhongshu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]黨與仲叔同郡，亦貞介士也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Dang was of the same commandery as Zhongshu, and was also a chaste and upright gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: Zhou Dang came from the same commandery as Min and was likewise celebrated for moral rigor.

sentences.zh: 見逸人傳。

translations.literal: See the Biography of Recluses.
translations.idiomatic: His story appears in the Traditions of Recluses.

sentences.zh: 皇甫謐高士傳曰：「黨見仲叔食無菜，遺之生蒜。

translations.literal: Huangfu Mi's Biographies of Lofty Gentlemen says: "Dang saw that Zhongshu ate without vegetables, and presented him with raw garlic.
translations.idiomatic: Huangfu Mi's Lives of Exalted Recluses records: "Seeing that Min's meals included no greens, Zhou sent him fresh garlic."

sentences.zh: 仲叔曰：『我欲省煩耳，今更作煩邪？

translations.literal: Zhongshu said: 'I wished to save trouble, only; now do you further make trouble?'
translations.idiomatic: Min replied, 'I was trying to keep life simple; are you now heaping fresh bother on me?'

sentences.zh: 』受而不食。」

translations.literal: ' He received it but did not eat it.""
translations.idiomatic: ' He took the gift but would not eat it."

sentences.zh: 注[三]勞其勤苦也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: It means they toiled his diligence and suffering.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: That is, Hou put him through pointless exertion.

sentences.zh: 勞音力到反。

translations.literal: For 勞 the reading is li-dao fan (incoming tone).
translations.idiomatic: The character 勞 is here read in the departing tone (lào).

sentences.zh: 注[四]案罪曰劾，自投其劾狀而去也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Investigating guilt is called he; it means he himself cast down his he document and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: He means a written accusation or resignation; he filed his own statement and left.

sentences.zh: 投猶下也。

translations.literal: To cast is like to lower.
translations.idiomatic: Here "cast" means "to submit" or "to drop off."

sentences.zh: 今有投辭、投牒之言也。

translations.literal: Now there are the sayings "cast a statement" and "cast a document."
translations.idiomatic: The same usage survives in phrases like "lodging a petition" or "filing a document."

sentences.zh: 仲叔同郡荀恁，字君大，[一]少亦修清節。

translations.literal: Xun Ren of the same commandery as Zhongshu, courtesy name Junda; [one] in youth he also cultivated pure integrity.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Ren of the same commandery as Min, styled Junda, was from his youth a student of austere integrity.

sentences.zh: 資財千萬，父越卒，悉散與九族。

translations.literal: His assets were in the millions; when his father Yue died, he entirely scattered them to the nine kinships.
translations.idiomatic: His family wealth ran into the millions, yet when his father Yue died he distributed the whole inheritance among his entire kindred.

sentences.zh: 隱居山澤，以求厥志。

translations.literal: He hid and dwelt in mountains and marshes, in order to seek his intent.
translations.idiomatic: He withdrew to the hills and wetlands to live out his chosen purpose.

sentences.zh: 王莽末，匈奴寇其本縣廣武，[二]聞恁名節，相約不入荀氏閭。

translations.literal: At the end of Wang Mang, the Xiongnu raided his native county Guangwu; [two] hearing Ren's fame for integrity, they mutually agreed not to enter the lanes of the Xun clan.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Near the end of Wang Mang's reign the Xiongnu raided Guangwu, his home county, but having heard of Xun Ren's reputation they pledged among themselves not to enter any lane where the Xuns lived.

sentences.zh: 光武征，以病不至。

translations.literal: Guangwu summoned him; because of illness he did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu summoned him; he pleaded illness and stayed away.

sentences.zh: 永平初，東平王蒼為驃騎將軍，開東合延賢俊，辟而應焉。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Yongping, the Prince of Dongping, Cang, was General of Agile Cavalry; he opened the eastern wing and extended to worthy and excellent men, and summoned him and he answered.
translations.idiomatic: Early in the Yongping era the Prince of Dongping, Liu Cang, served as general-in-chief of agile cavalry; he opened his eastern hall to men of talent and summoned Xun, who this time accepted.

sentences.zh: 及後朝會，顯宗戲之曰：「先帝征君不至，驃騎辟君而來，何也？」

translations.literal: Later at court assembly, Emperor Xianzong jested with him, saying: "The former emperor summoned you and you did not come; the General of Agile Cavalry summoned you and you came—why?"
translations.idiomatic: Later, at a court audience, Emperor Ming teased him: "My late father called you and you stayed home; the general-in-chief calls and you appear—what is the difference?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「先帝秉德以惠下，故臣可得不來。

translations.literal: He replied, saying: "The former emperor grasped virtue in order to show favor to those below; therefore your subject could obtain not to come.
translations.idiomatic: He answered: "The late emperor ruled by moral power and let his subjects choose their own path, so I was free to stay away."

sentences.zh: 驃騎執法以檢下，[三]故臣不敢不至。

translations.literal: The General of Agile Cavalry holds the law in order to examine those below; [three] therefore your subject dared not not to come.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] The general-in-chief enforces regulations and keeps his staff in line, so I dared not refuse."

sentences.zh: 」後月餘，罷歸，卒於家。

translations.literal: " After more than a month he was dismissed and returned, and died at home.
translations.idiomatic: " A little over a month later he was released from office and went home, where he died.

sentences.zh: 注[一]恁音而甚反。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: For 恁 the reading is er-shen fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The name 恁 is read like rěn (er-shen fan).

sentences.zh: 注[二]廣武，縣，屬太原郡，故城在今代州鴈門縣也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Guangwu is a county belonging to Taiyuan commandery; the old walled city is in today's Yanmen county of Dai prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: Guangwu was a county in Taiyuan commandery; its old seat lies in present-day Yanmen County, Shanxi.

sentences.zh: 注[三]檢猶察也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: the word glossed means to examine, like 'inspect'.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: In this gloss the verb means to scrutinize or keep watch.

sentences.zh: 桓帝時，安陽人魏桓，字仲英，亦數被征。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Huan, Wei Huan of Anyang, courtesy name Zhongying, was also several times summoned.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Huan, Wei Huan of Anyang, styled Zhongying, was repeatedly summoned to office.

sentences.zh: 其鄉人勸之行。

translations.literal: His fellow villagers urged him to go.
translations.idiomatic: Neighbors and friends urged him to answer the call.

sentences.zh: 桓曰：「夫干祿求進，所以行其志也。

translations.literal: Huan said: "Now seeking emolument and seeking advancement is the means by which to carry out one's intent.
translations.idiomatic: Wei replied: "One takes office and seeks promotion precisely in order to put one's principles into practice."

sentences.zh: 今後宮千數，其可損乎？

translations.literal: Now the rear palace is in the thousands—can it be reduced?"
translations.idiomatic: The inner palace already holds thousands of women—could I really reduce their number?"

sentences.zh: 廄馬萬匹，其可減乎？

translations.literal: The stable horses are ten thousand head—can they be decreased?"
translations.idiomatic: The imperial stables hold ten thousand horses—could I slash that herd?"

sentences.zh: 左右悉權豪，其可去乎？

translations.literal: Those at left and right are all powerful magnates—can they be removed?"
translations.idiomatic: Every man at the ruler's elbow is a powerful favorite—could I sweep them all away?"

sentences.zh: 」皆對曰：「不可。

translations.literal: " All answered saying: "It cannot."
translations.idiomatic: " They all said no, it could not be done.

sentences.zh: 」桓乃慨然歎曰：「使桓生行死歸，於諸子何有哉！

translations.literal: " Huan then sighed with feeling, saying: "If it makes Huan live going and die returning—what is there in it for you gentlemen!"
translations.idiomatic: " Wei cried out in despair: "If my going to court means I march in alive and am carried out dead, what good does that do any of you?!"

sentences.zh: 」[一]遂隱身不出。

translations.literal: " [One] Thereupon he hid his person and did not go out.
translations.idiomatic: " [One] He withdrew from the world and never took office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]若忤時強諫，死而後歸，於諸勸行者復何益也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: If by opposing the times and forcibly remonstrating, dying and afterward returning—what further benefit is there to those who urged going.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: If he had defied the age with blunt remonstrance and come home only as a corpse, his death would have done his well-wishers no good at all.

sentences.zh: 若二三子，可謂識去就之□，候時而處。

translations.literal: As for you two or three gentlemen, it can be said you know the [lacuna] of going and staying, and await the time and place yourselves.
translations.idiomatic: Men such as these understood when to serve and when to withdraw; they watched the times and chose their ground.

sentences.zh: [一]夫然，豈其枯槁苟而已哉？

translations.literal: [One] Being so, how could it be that they were withered and haggard and careless only?
translations.idiomatic: [One] Surely they were not mere stick-in-the-mud recluses hiding from the world?

sentences.zh: 蓋詭時審己，以成其道焉。

translations.literal: Generally they deceived the times and examined themselves, thereby completing their way.
translations.idiomatic: They bent with the age, took their own measure, and so brought their moral path to fulfillment.

sentences.zh: [二]余故列其風流，區而載之。

translations.literal: [Two] Therefore I arrange their elegant currents, distinguish and record them.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] I therefore set out their several temperaments and record each in its own place.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]□，節也。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one]: [lacuna] means integrity.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] Note one: The missing graph denotes moral integrity or measure.

sentences.zh: 候時以居，不失去就也。

translations.literal: Awaiting the time thereby to dwell, not losing going and staying.
translations.idiomatic: They timed their withdrawal or advance and never mistook which course to take.

sentences.zh: 注[二]詭，違也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: the glossed word means to deviate or oppose.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: Here it means to go against the grain or depart from the norm.

translations.literal: An asterisk appears alone in the received text at this point.
translations.idiomatic: The edition marks a break or textual note with an asterisk.

translations.literal: The graphs "(also)" in parentheses.
translations.idiomatic: A marginal gloss "(also)" survives in the apparatus.

sentences.zh: **[多]*若違時，志存量己也。

translations.literal: **[many]* if opposing the times, the intent preserves and measures oneself.
translations.idiomatic: The damaged line appears to continue the gloss: when a man sets himself against the fashion of the day, he keeps his purpose clear and takes his own measure.

sentences.zh: 注[三]言其清潔之風，各有條流，故區別而紀之。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: It speaks of their pure and clean winds, each having its channels and currents; therefore I distinguish and record them.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: Each man's spotless ethos follows its own channel, which is why the historian sorts them under separate headings.

sentences.zh: 周燮字彥祖，汝南安城人，*

translations.literal: Zhou Xie, courtesy name Yanzu, was a man of Ancheng in Runan, *
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Xie, styled Yanzu, came from Ancheng in Runan commandery (the line breaks off in the received text).

translations.literal: The graphs "(law)" in parentheses.
translations.idiomatic: A corrupted gloss "(法)" stands where the text should read 決 (judicial).

sentences.zh: **[決]*曹掾燕之後也。

translations.literal: **[decide]* clerk of the Judicial Bureau, a descendant of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: He was descended from Yan, who had served as an attendant clerk in the Judicial Bureau—a line damaged in the manuscript.

sentences.zh: [一]燮生而欽頤折頞，丑狀駭人。

translations.literal: [One] Xie was born with a drawn-in chin and a bent bridge of the nose; his ugly appearance startled people.
translations.idiomatic: [One] From birth his face was shockingly odd, with a receding chin and a crooked nose.

sentences.zh: [二] 其母欲□之，其父不聽，曰：「吾聞賢聖多有異貌。

translations.literal: [Two] His mother wished to [lacuna] him; his father would not listen, saying: "I have heard that worthies and sages often have unusual appearances.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] His mother wanted to abandon the child, but his father forbade her, saying, "The wise often wear strange faces."

sentences.zh: [三]興我宗者，乃此兒也。」

translations.literal: [Three] The one who will raise up our lineage is this boy.""
translations.idiomatic: [Three] This boy will be the one to bring honor to our house.""

sentences.zh: 於是養之。

translations.literal: Thereupon they raised him.
translations.idiomatic: So they kept the child and reared him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]燕具獨行篇周嘉傳。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Yan is fully in the Biography of Zhou Jia in the Solitary Conduct scroll.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The ancestry of the clerk Yan is detailed in the biography of Zhou Jia under "Solitary Conduct."

sentences.zh: 注[二]頤，頷也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: the jaw graph denotes the chin.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: In this gloss it means the lower jaw or chin.

sentences.zh: 欽頤，曲頷也。

translations.literal: Drawn-in chin means a bent chin.
translations.idiomatic: "Drawn-in chin" describes a receding or sharply angled jaw.

sentences.zh: 說文曰：「頞，鼻莖也。

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: the nose-bridge word means the stem of the nose.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen dictionary defines the nose-bridge graph as the bridge of the nose.

sentences.zh: 」折亦曲也。

translations.literal: " Bent is also curved.
translations.idiomatic: " Here the verb for 'bend' likewise means bent or crooked.

sentences.zh: 欽音丘凡反。

translations.literal: For 欽 the reading is qiu-fan fan.
translations.idiomatic: The graph 欽 is read qīn (qiu-fan fan).

sentences.zh: 欽或作「顩」，音同。

translations.literal: 欽 is sometimes written 顩, with the same pronunciation.
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts write 顩 for 欽; the pronunciation is the same.

sentences.zh: 注[三]伏羲牛首，女媧蛇軀，戲繇鳥喙，孔子牛唇，是聖賢異貌也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Fuxi had an ox head, Nüwa a serpent body, Xiyao a bird beak, Confucius ox lips—these are unusual appearances of sages and worthies.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: Fuxi was pictured with an ox's head, Nüwa with a serpent's body, Gao Yao with a beak like a bird's, Confucius with lips like a cow's—tradition often gives sages singular faces.

sentences.zh: 又蔡澤亦顩頤蹙頞。

translations.literal: Moreover Cai Ze also had a drawn-in chin and a wrinkled bridge of the nose.
translations.idiomatic: Cai Ze too was famous for a sunken chin and a deeply furrowed nose.

sentences.zh: 始在髫鬌，而知廉讓；

translations.literal: From the time he was in hanging hair tufts, he knew modest yielding;
translations.idiomatic: Even in childhood, with his hair still in boyish knots, he showed a sense of modesty and deference.

sentences.zh: [一]十歲就學，能通詩、論；

translations.literal: [One] At ten sui he went to study and was able to penetrate the Odes and the Analects;
translations.idiomatic: [One] At ten he began formal schooling and soon mastered the Classic of Odes and the Analects.

sentences.zh: 及長，專精禮、易。

translations.literal: When grown, he specialized in the Rites and the Changes.
translations.idiomatic: As an adult he devoted himself to the Ritual canon and the Book of Changes.

sentences.zh: 不讀非聖之書，不修賀問之好。

translations.literal: He did not read books that were not of sages; he did not cultivate the fondness for congratulatory visiting.
translations.idiomatic: He refused to open any work not authored by sages and took no pleasure in social calls and polite notes.

sentences.zh: 有先人草廬結於罔畔，[二]下有陂田，常肆勤以自給。

translations.literal: There was an ancestral thatched hut tied at the edge of the net [ridge]; [two] below there was sloping field; he constantly spread diligence to supply himself.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] His family still owned a rough hut on a ridge above the marsh, with terraced fields below, and he worked those plots tirelessly to support himself.

sentences.zh: [三]非身所耕漁，則不食也。

translations.literal: [Three] What was not personally ploughed or fished by himself, then he did not eat.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] He would eat nothing that he had not grown or caught with his own hands.

sentences.zh: 鄉黨宗族希得見者。

translations.literal: In village, community, and clan, those who seldom could see him.
translations.idiomatic: Neighbors and kinsmen rarely caught a glimpse of him.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]髫，發也。

translations.literal: [Four] Commentary [one]: Tiao means hair.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] Note one: The gloss explains the childhood forelock as simply "hair" in the ritual sense.

sentences.zh: 禮記曰：「子生三月之末，擇日翦發為*

translations.literal: The Record of Rites says: "At the end of the third month after birth, they choose a day to clip the hair into *
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Rites says that when an infant completes its third month, the family picks a day to trim the hair into the prescribed style (the line breaks in the received text).

translations.literal: The gloss in parentheses supplies the reading for the childhood tuft.
translations.idiomatic: A marginal gloss identifies the graph as the one for the ritual forelock.

sentences.zh: **[鬌]*，男角女羈，否則男左女右。

translations.literal: **[tuo]* for boys the horn tufts, for girls the bridle tufts; if not, then boys left and girls right.
translations.idiomatic: The damaged continuation prescribes the tuo style: for boys the "horn" knots, for girls the "bridle" knots, or else the boy's knot on the left and the girl's on the right.

sentences.zh: 」鬌音徒果反。

translations.literal: " For tuo the reading is tuo-guo fan (entering tone).
translations.idiomatic: " The character for the back tuft is read in the entering tone, tuo-guo fan.

sentences.zh: 注[二]山脊曰岡。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: A mountain ridge is called a gang.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: A mountain crest is what the text calls a gang, a ridge.

sentences.zh: 注[三]肆，陳也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Si means to array.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: Here "spread" means to set forth or display, as in arranging work.

sentences.zh: 注[四]謝承書曰「燮居家清處，非法不言，兄弟、父子、室家相待如賓，鄉曲不善者皆從其教」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Xie Cheng's book says: "Xie dwelt at home in pure retirement; he spoke nothing contrary to law; brothers, fathers and sons, and the inner household treated one another as guest and host; those in the hamlet who were not good all followed his instruction."
translations.idiomatic: Note four: Xie Cheng records that Zhou Xie kept a scrupulous household, broke no rule in speech, treated kin like honored guests, and even neighbors of poor character submitted to his example.

sentences.zh: 舉孝廉、賢良方正，特徵，皆以疾辭，延光二年，安帝以玄纁羔幣聘燮，[一] 及南陽馮良，二郡各遣丞掾致禮。

translations.literal: Recommended Filial and Incorrupt, Worthy and Upright, specially summoned—he all declined citing illness. In the second year of Yanguang, Emperor An engaged Xie with dark-red silk, lamb, and cash gifts, [one] and Feng Liang of Nanyang; the two commanderies each sent an assistant magistrate and clerk to deliver the ceremony.
translations.idiomatic: Though repeatedly recommended as filial and incorrupt, as worthy and upright, and by special summons, he pleaded illness each time. In 123 CE Emperor An sent the full ceremonial bundle of black and crimson silks, a lamb, and currency to hire both Zhou Xie and Feng Liang of Nanyang, and the two commanderies each dispatched officials to present the court's respect.

sentences.zh: 宗族更勸之曰：「夫修德立行，所以為國。

translations.literal: The lineage repeatedly urged him, saying: "Now cultivating virtue and establishing conduct is the means by which to serve the state.
translations.idiomatic: His kinsmen kept urging him: "You build character and establish a name precisely so you may serve the realm."

sentences.zh: 自先世以來，勳寵相承，君獨何為守東岡之陂乎？

translations.literal: From former generations, merit and favour have succeeded one another; why alone, sir, do you keep to the slope on the eastern ridge?"
translations.idiomatic: For generations our house has won honor after honor from the throne—why must you alone cling to that eastern hillside plot?"

sentences.zh: 」燮曰：「吾既不能隱處巢穴，追綺季之跡，[二]而猶顯然不遠父母之國，斯固以滑泥揚波，同其流矣。

translations.literal: " Xie said: "I can neither hide and dwell in a nest nor trace the footprints of Qi Ji; [two] yet I still conspicuously do not leave far the land of my father and mother—this is surely to slide in the mud and raise the waves, sharing their current.
translations.idiomatic: " Zhou replied: "I cannot burrow into a hermit's den like the Four Hoary Heads of Shang Mountain, [two] yet I still refuse to leave my native soil; that already means wallowing in the same mud and riding the same tide as the world."

sentences.zh: [三] 夫修道者，度其時而動。

translations.literal: [Three] Now the man who cultivates the Way measures the times and then moves.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] A man who cultivates the Way weighs the season before he stirs.

sentences.zh: 動而不時，焉得亨乎！

translations.literal: To move without timeliness—how can he obtain heng!
translations.idiomatic: Act out of season and how can anything go smoothly for you?!"

sentences.zh: 」[四] 因自載到穎川陽城，遣*[門]*生送敬，遂辭疾而歸。

translations.literal: " [Four] Thereupon he had himself driven to Yangcheng in Yingchuan, sent a student to present respects, then declined on grounds of illness and returned.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] He then rode in his own carriage to Yangcheng in Yingchuan commandery, had a disciple deliver his formal thanks, pleaded illness, and went home.

sentences.zh: [五]良亦載病到近縣，送禮而還。

translations.literal: [Five] Liang also rode, claiming illness, to a nearby county, returned the gifts and came back.
translations.idiomatic: [Five] Feng Liang likewise travelled to a neighboring county, feigned illness, returned the imperial gifts, and withdrew.

sentences.zh: [六]詔書告二郡，歲以羊酒養病。

translations.literal: [Six] An edict informed the two commanderies to supply yearly lamb and wine for nurturing illness.
translations.idiomatic: [Six] An edict instructed both commanderies to send them lamb and wine every year while they convalesced.

sentences.zh: 注[一]禮，卿執羔。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: By ritual, a minister takes a lamb.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Rites prescribe that a minister present a lamb as his credential gift.

sentences.zh: 董仲舒春秋繁露曰：「凡贊卿用羔，羔有角而不用，類仁者；

translations.literal: Dong Zhongshu's Luxuriant Dew of the Spring and Autumn says: "Generally the gift a minister presents is a lamb; a lamb has horns yet does not use them, like one who is humane;
translations.idiomatic: Dong Zhongshu's Luxuriant Dew explains: "The minister's offering is always a lamb: it has horns but does not gore, like the humane man who restrains force."

sentences.zh: 執之不鳴，殺之不縪，類死義者；

translations.literal: when seized it does not cry, when killed it is not bound—like one who dies for duty;
translations.idiomatic: Seized, it does not bleat; led to slaughter it needs no rope—like one who meets death for a righteous cause."

sentences.zh: 羔飲其母必跪，類知禮者：故以為贄。」

translations.literal: the lamb kneels when it suckles its dam, like one who knows ritual; therefore it is taken as the gift.""
translations.idiomatic: It kneels to nurse its mother, like one who understands ritual—hence it serves as the proper gift.""

sentences.zh: 注[二]綺季、東園公、夏黃公、□裡先生，謂之四皓，隱於商山。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Qi Ji, Lord Dongyuan, Lord Xiahua Huang, and the gentleman of [lacuna]-li—these are called the Four Hoary Heads; they hid on Mount Shang.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: Qi Ji, Lord Dongyuan, Lord Xiahua Huang, and Master Luli—the "Four Hoary Heads"—withdrew to Mount Shang (the fourth name is damaged in some editions).

sentences.zh: 見前書也。

translations.literal: See the former book.
translations.idiomatic: Their story appears in the earlier Han history.

sentences.zh: 注[三]滑，混也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Hua means to mix.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: "Slide" here means to wallow or blend with, to mix in.

sentences.zh: 楚詞：「何不滑其泥而揚其波。

translations.literal: Chu ci: "Why not slide in its mud and raise its waves."
translations.idiomatic: The Songs of Chu asks: "Why not smear yourself with that mud and ride its rising waves?".

sentences.zh: 」滑音古沒反。

translations.literal: " For hua the reading is gu-mo fan (entering tone).
translations.idiomatic: " The verb "slide" is read gu-mo fan, in the entering tone.

sentences.zh: 注[四]亨，通也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Heng means unobstructed.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: "Heng" in the sense of success means unobstructed passage.

sentences.zh: 書曰：「慮善以動，動惟厥時。」

translations.literal: The Documents say: "Consider what is good and then move; movement respects its season.""
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Documents says: "Plan for the good before you act, and let every action suit its season.""

sentences.zh: 注[五]送敬猶致謝也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Presenting respects is like conveying thanks.
translations.idiomatic: Note five: "Sending respects" means formally expressing gratitude.

sentences.zh: 注[六]送禮謂送其所致之禮也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Returning the gift means returning what was brought as gift.
translations.idiomatic: Note six: "Returning the gifts" means sending back exactly what the envoys had delivered.

sentences.zh: 良字君郎。

translations.literal: Liang's courtesy name was Junlang.
translations.idiomatic: Feng Liang's courtesy name was Junlang.

sentences.zh: 出於孤微，少作縣吏。

translations.literal: He came from orphan and humble origins; in youth he served as a county clerk.
translations.idiomatic: He rose from obscure and humble beginnings and began as a petty county clerk.

sentences.zh: 年三十，為尉從佐。

translations.literal: At thirty sui he was an assistant accompanying the district captain.
translations.idiomatic: At thirty he became an attendant aide to the district captain.

sentences.zh: [一]奉檄迎督郵，即路慨然，恥在□役，[二]因壞車殺馬，毀裂衣冠，乃遁至犍為，從杜撫學。

translations.literal: [One] Bearing a dispatch to welcome the touring inspector, once on the road he sighed deeply, ashamed to be in [lacuna] service; [two] therefore he smashed his cart, killed his horse, tore cap and gown, fled to Qianwei, and studied under Du Fu.
translations.idiomatic: [One] Ordered to carry a summons and meet the touring inspector, he set out, then burned with shame at running errands for petty officials; [two] he wrecked his cart, slew his horse, shredded his cap and robes, fled to Qianwei commandery, and became a pupil of Du Fu.

sentences.zh: 妻子求索，蹤多斷絕。

translations.literal: Wife and children searched for him; the trail often broke off.
translations.idiomatic: His wife and children searched for him but repeatedly lost his trail.

sentences.zh: 後乃見草中有敗車死馬，衣裳腐朽，謂為虎狼盜賊所害，發喪制服。

translations.literal: Later they saw in the grass a wrecked cart and dead horse, clothes rotten, and thought tigers, wolves, or bandits had harmed him; they held mourning and wore mourning dress.
translations.idiomatic: Later they found a smashed cart, a dead horse, and rotted clothing in the grass and assumed robbers or beasts had killed him; the family mourned him as dead.

sentences.zh: 積十許年，乃還鄉裡。

translations.literal: Accumulating more than ten years, then he returned to his village.
translations.idiomatic: Only after more than a decade did he come back to his home district.

sentences.zh: 志行高整，非禮不動，遇妻子如君臣，鄉黨以為儀表。

translations.literal: His purpose and conduct were lofty and composed; he did not move contrary to ritual; he treated wife and children as ruler and ministers; village and community took him as a model and measure.
translations.idiomatic: His aims and deportment were austere and exacting: he never stirred except by ritual, treated wife and children with the gravity due ruler and subject, and the whole community looked to him as a standard.

sentences.zh: 燮、良年皆七十餘終。

translations.literal: Xie and Liang both ended at more than seventy sui.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Xie and Feng Liang both lived past seventy before they died.

sentences.zh: 注[一]從佐謂隨從而已，不主案牘也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Assistant accompanying means following in attendance only, not presiding over documents.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: "Attendant aide" means he merely followed the captain about and did not handle paperwork.

sentences.zh: 注[二]廝，賤也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Si means base.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The word glossed means menial or lowly service.

sentences.zh: 黃憲字叔度，汝南慎陽人也。

translations.literal: Huang Xian, courtesy name Shudu, was a man of Shenyang in Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Xian, styled Shudu, was a native of Shenyang in Runan commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]世貧賤，父為牛醫。

translations.literal: [One] For generations poor and base; his father was an ox doctor.
translations.idiomatic: [One] His family had long been poor and low in rank; his father treated cattle for a living.

sentences.zh: 注[一]在慎水之南，因以名縣。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It lies south of the Shen River; therefore by it the county was named.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The county took its name from its position south of the Shen River.

sentences.zh: 南陽有順陽國，而流俗書此或作「順陽」者，誤。

translations.literal: Nanyang had the state of Shunyang, but vulgar copies sometimes write this as "Shunyang"—it is an error.
translations.idiomatic: Nanyang did contain a princedom called Shunyang, and careless copyists sometimes substitute that name here by mistake.

sentences.zh: 穎川荀淑至慎陽，遇憲於逆旅，[一]時年十四，淑竦然異之，揖與語，移日不能去。

translations.literal: Xun Shu of Yingchuan arrived at Shenyang and met Xian at a travelling inn; [one] he was then fourteen sui. Shu was startled and amazed; he bowed and spoke with him and could not leave for a shifted day.
translations.idiomatic: When Xun Shu of Yingchuan passed through Shenyang he met Huang Xian, then only fourteen, at an inn; [one] Xun was so struck that he bowed, talked with him the whole day through, and could not tear himself away.

sentences.zh: 謂憲曰：「子，吾之師表也。

translations.literal: He said to Xian: "You are my teacher and model.
translations.idiomatic: He told Huang, "You are the man I take as my teacher and pattern."

sentences.zh: 」既而前至袁*

translations.literal: " Soon after he went forward and arrived at Yuan *
translations.idiomatic: " Presently he travelled on to call on Yuan (the name breaks in the manuscript).

translations.literal: The gloss in parentheses gives the personal name Hong.
translations.idiomatic: The marginal gloss identifies the host as Yuan Hong.

sentences.zh: **[閬]*[二]所，未及勞問，逆曰：「子國有顏子，寧識之乎？

translations.literal: **[Lang]*[two] place; before he reached polite inquiries, he reversed and said: "Your state has a Yan Zi—do you perhaps know him?"
translations.idiomatic: [Two] At Yuan Lang's house, before the usual pleasantries, his host blurted out, "Your commandery has its own Yan Hui—surely you know the man?"

translations.literal: " [Three]*
translations.idiomatic: " End of quoted speech; commentary tag three (text damaged).

translations.literal: The gloss in parentheses reads Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Another gloss confirms the name Hong.

sentences.zh: **[閬]*曰：「見吾叔度邪？」

translations.literal: **[Lang]* said: "Have you seen my Shudu?""
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong continued, "Then you have met Huang Shudu?""

sentences.zh: 是時，同郡戴良才高倨□，而見憲未嘗不正容，及歸，惘然若有失也。

translations.literal: At that time Dai Liang of the same commandery was talented and high and haughty [lacuna], yet when he saw Xian he never failed to straighten his expression; when he returned, he was dazed as if he had lost something.
translations.idiomatic: Dai Liang of the same commandery was brilliant but arrogant, yet in Huang Xian's presence he always composed his face; returning home he would look dazed, as though something had been taken from him.

sentences.zh: 其母問曰：「汝復從牛醫兒來邪？

translations.literal: His mother asked, saying: "Have you again come from the ox-doctor's son?"
translations.idiomatic: His mother asked, "Have you been visiting the cattle doctor's boy again?"

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「良不見叔度，不自以為不及；

translations.literal: " He replied, saying: "Until Liang does not see Shudu, he does not himself think he falls short;
translations.idiomatic: " He answered, "Before I met Shudu I never realized how far short I fell."

sentences.zh: 既鶯其人，則瞻之在前，忽焉在後，[四]固難得而測矣。

translations.literal: Once [lacuna: graph often read as 'behold'] that man, then looking at him he is in front, suddenly he is behind—[four] truly hard to obtain and measure.
translations.idiomatic: Once you stand before him he seems now ahead of you, now behind—[four] he slips from every attempt to take his measure (the verb is corrupt in the received text)."

sentences.zh: 」同郡陳蕃、周舉常相謂曰：「時月之閒不見黃生，則鄙吝之萌復存乎心。

translations.literal: " Chen Fan and Zhou Ju of the same commandery often said to each other: "If in the space of seasons and months we do not see Master Huang, then the sprouts of baseness and stinginess again exist in the heart.
translations.idiomatic: " Chen Fan and Zhou Ju used to tell each other, "If we go a month without seeing Master Huang, coarse and grasping thoughts creep back into the mind."

sentences.zh: 」[五]及蕃為三公，臨朝歎曰：「叔度若在，吾不敢先佩印綬矣。

translations.literal: " [Five] When Fan became one of the Three Dukes, facing court he sighed, saying: "If Shudu were alive, I would not dare first to wear the seal-ribbons.
translations.idiomatic: [Five] When Chen Fan rose to one of the three highest offices he said at court, "If Shudu were still alive, I would never dare tie on these ribbons of rank before he did."

sentences.zh: 」太守王龔在郡，禮進賢達，多所降致，卒不能屈憲。

translations.literal: " When Prefect Wang Gong was in the commandery, he courteously advanced worthy and accomplished men, many he brought down and summoned, yet in the end could not bend Xian.
translations.idiomatic: " Prefect Wang Gong courted worthy men far and wide, yet he could never persuade Huang Xian to serve."

sentences.zh: 郭林宗少游汝南，先過袁*

translations.literal: Guo Linzong in youth travelled Runan, first passed Yuan *
translations.idiomatic: In his youth Guo Tai toured Runan and first called on Yuan (name damaged).

translations.literal: The gloss reads Hong.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss identifies him as Yuan Hong.

sentences.zh: **[閬]*，不宿而退；

translations.literal: **[Lang]*, he did not lodge overnight but withdrew;
translations.idiomatic: At Yuan Lang's gate he would not stay the night and left at once;

sentences.zh: 進往從憲，累日方還。

translations.literal: he went forward to follow Xian, accumulated days then returned.
translations.idiomatic: yet when he went on to Huang Xian he lingered many days before he would go home.

sentences.zh: 或以問林宗。

translations.literal: Someone used this to ask Linzong.
translations.idiomatic: Someone asked Guo Tai to explain the contrast.

sentences.zh: [六]林宗曰：「奉高之器，譬諸*

translations.literal: [Six] Linzong said: "Fenggao's capacity compares to *
translations.idiomatic: [Six] Guo Tai replied, "Yuan Fenggao's talent is like (the simile breaks in the text).

translations.literal: The gloss reads fan.
translations.idiomatic: A gloss suggests the word "fan" (overflowing stream).

sentences.zh: **[氿]*濫，雖清而易挹。

translations.literal: **[gui]* overflow; although clear yet easy to ladle.
translations.idiomatic: The gui spring's overflow is bright enough, yet shallow enough to dip with a cup.

sentences.zh: [七] 叔度汪汪若千頃陂，澄之不清，淆之不濁，不可量也。

translations.literal: [Seven] Shudu is vast and overflowing like a thousand-qing marsh: clarify it and it does not clear; stir it and it does not turn turbid—cannot be measured.
translations.idiomatic: [Seven] Shudu is like a thousand acres of still water: you cannot make it clearer by settling it, nor muddier by stirring—no one can sound his depth."

sentences.zh: 」[八] 注[一]逆旅，客舍。

translations.literal: " [Eight] Commentary [one]: A travelling inn is a guest lodging.
translations.idiomatic: " [Eight] Note one: "Travelling inn" means a public hostel.

sentences.zh: 注[二]一作「閬」。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: One version writes "Lang".
translations.idiomatic: Note two: One manuscript reads the name as Lang instead.

sentences.zh: 注[三]顏子，顏回也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Yan Zi is Yan Hui.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: "Yan Zi" means Yan Hui, Confucius's foremost disciple.

sentences.zh: 注[四]論語顏回慕孔子之言也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: It is Yan Hui's words admiring Confucius in the Analects.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: The image echoes Yan Hui's praise of Confucius in the Analects.

sentences.zh: 注[五]吝，貪也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Lin means grasping.
translations.idiomatic: Note five: "Stinginess" here means a grasping, petty mind.

sentences.zh: 注[六]郭泰別傳曰：「時林宗過薛恭祖，恭祖問曰：『聞足不見袁奉高，車不停軌，鑾不輟軶，從叔度乃彌信宿也？

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: Guo Tai's Separate Biography says: "When Linzong passed Xue Gongzu, Gongzu asked, saying: 'I hear you, sir, do not see Yuan Fenggao—the carriage does not halt its tracks, the bells do not stop their yoke; yet for Shudu you extend to two full nights?'
translations.idiomatic: Note six: Guo Tai's unofficial biography records Xue Gongzu asking him why he would not spend a single night with Yuan Fenggao yet stayed two nights with Huang Shudu?

sentences.zh: 』」注[七]奉高，閎字也。

translations.literal: '"" Commentary [seven]: Fenggao is Hong's courtesy name.
translations.idiomatic: '"" Note seven: Fenggao was Yuan Hong's style."

sentences.zh: 爾雅曰：「側出*

translations.literal: The Erya says: "Issuing from the side *
translations.idiomatic: The Erya defines a spring that issues from the side as—

translations.literal: The gloss reads fan.
translations.idiomatic: the gloss gives the reading "fan."

sentences.zh: **[氿]*泉，正出濫泉。

translations.literal: **[gui]* spring; upright issue is overflowing spring.
translations.idiomatic: The gui spring issues from the side, while a vertical jet is called an overflowing spring."
" (The quotation breaks off in the edition.)

translations.literal: The gloss reads fan.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss "fan" is repeated.

translations.literal: **[gui]* is read gui.
translations.idiomatic: The graph gui is read like the word for "track" (gui).

sentences.zh: 濫音檻。

translations.literal: For lan the reading is lan (like the word for threshold).
translations.idiomatic: The second syllable lan is read like the word for "threshold."

sentences.zh: 注[八]淆，混也。

translations.literal: Commentary [eight]: Xiao means to mix.
translations.idiomatic: Note eight: "Stir" here means to muddy or blend.

sentences.zh: 憲初舉孝廉，又辟公府，友人勸其仕，憲亦不拒之，暫到京師而還，竟無所就。

translations.literal: Xian was at first recommended Filial and Incorrupt, again summoned to the Duke's office; friends urged him to serve; Xian also did not reject them; he briefly arrived at the capital and returned, and in the end took nothing.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Xian was recommended as filial and incorrupt and summoned to the high minister's office; friends pressed him to accept, and he did not flatly refuse—he went once to the capital, then came home and never took a post.

sentences.zh: 年四十八終，天下號曰「征君」。

translations.literal: At forty-eight sui he ended; the world styled him "the summoned gentleman.""
translations.idiomatic: He died at forty-eight, and all who heard of him called him "the gentleman who was summoned but never served.""

sentences.zh: 論曰：黃憲言論風旨，無所傳聞，然士君子見之者，靡不服深遠，去玼吝。

translations.literal: Discourse says: Of Huang Xian's language and teachings and style and intent, nothing is transmitted and heard; yet gentlemen who saw him, none failed to submit to depth and distance, removing ci stinginess.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's judgment: Not a line of Huang Xian's conversation survives, yet every educated man who met him bowed to a depth he could not fathom and shed petty meanness from his heart.

sentences.zh: [一] 將以道周性全，無德而稱乎？

translations.literal: [One] Was it by the Way completing and nature whole, without virtue yet titled?"
translations.idiomatic: [One] Was it because the Way had filled out his nature until he possessed a virtue too large to name?"

sentences.zh: [二]余曾祖穆侯[三]以為憲隤然其處順，[四]淵乎其似道，[五]淺深莫臻其分，清濁未議其方。

translations.literal: [Two] My great-great-grandfather Mu Marquis [three] thought that Xian was yielding in his dwelling in compliance; [four] profound as though like the Way; [five] shallow and deep could not reach his portion, clear and turbid had not yet debated his square.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] My great-great-grandfather Marquis Mu [three] believed Huang Xian rested in perfect compliance with circumstance, [four] seemed bottomless as the Way itself, [five] so that neither shallow nor deep minds could reach his measure, nor could any clear distinction of pure and tainted define him.

sentences.zh: [六]若及門於孔氏，其殆庶乎！

translations.literal: [Six] If reaching the gate of the Kong clan, he perhaps nearly was among those who almost had it!
translations.idiomatic: [Six] Had he walked through Confucius's gate, he might well have been numbered among those who "almost had it!""

sentences.zh: [七]故嘗著論雲。

translations.literal: [Seven] Therefore I once set forth a discourse.
translations.idiomatic: [Seven] That is why he once wrote an essay on the subject.

sentences.zh: 注[一]玼音此。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: For ci the reading is ci.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The character read "ci" is glossed with the same sound as "this."

sentences.zh: 說文曰：「鮮色也。

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Fresh colour.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines the graph as "fresh color" or "lustre."

sentences.zh: 」據此文當為「疵」，作「玼」者，古字通也。

translations.literal: " According to this text it ought to be 'blemish'; writing 'ci' is ancient graphs communicating.
translations.idiomatic: " In this passage it should mean "blemish"; the alternate spelling reflects an old graphic interchange."

sentences.zh: 注[二]道周備，性全一。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Way is complete and prepared; nature is whole and one.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Way had rounded him out and his inborn nature stood whole and unified.

sentences.zh: 無德而稱，言其德大無能名焉。

translations.literal: "Without virtue yet titled" means his virtue was great and none could name it.
translations.idiomatic: "Without virtue yet titled" means his moral power was too vast for any name.

sentences.zh: 注[三]晉書曰：「范汪字玄平，安北將軍，謚曰穆侯。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Jin History says: "Fan Wang, courtesy name Xuanping, was General Who Guards the North, posthumous title Mu Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: The Jin History records Fan Wang, styled Xuanping, general who pacified the north, enfeoffed posthumously as Marquis Mu.

sentences.zh: 汪生寧，寧生泰，泰生曄。」

translations.literal: Wang sired Ning, Ning sired Tai, Tai sired Ye.""
translations.idiomatic: Fan Wang fathered Fan Ning, who fathered Fan Tai, who fathered Fan Ye, the compiler of this history.""

sentences.zh: 注[四]易系詞曰：「坤隤然示人簡矣。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: The Changes, Xi ci says: "Earth shows itself yielding and displays simplicity to people.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: The Appended Remarks to the Book of Changes say of the hexagram Earth that it "lies soft and open, showing men simplicity."

sentences.zh: 」隤，柔順貌。

translations.literal: " Tui is the appearance of yielding softness.
translations.idiomatic: " The word rendered "yielding" describes a gentle, supple bearing."

sentences.zh: 注[五]老子曰：「道沖而用之，或不盈，淵乎似萬物之宗。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Laozi says: "The Way is empty yet in using it, perhaps it is not full; profound! It seems the ancestor of the myriad things."
translations.idiomatic: Note five: Laozi says the Way is an empty vessel that never runs dry, deep as the source of all things.

sentences.zh: 」言淵深不可知也。

translations.literal: " It means profound depth and unknowability.
translations.idiomatic: " The gloss stresses how unfathomably deep such a person is."

sentences.zh: 注[六]廣雅曰：「方，所也。」

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The Guangya says: "Fang means place.""
translations.idiomatic: Note six: The Guangya defines fang as "place" or "quarter.""

sentences.zh: 注[七]易系詞曰：「顏氏之子，其殆庶幾乎！

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: The Changes, Xi ci says: "The son of the Yan clan—perhaps he nearly had it!"
translations.idiomatic: Note seven: The Appended Remarks cite Confucius on Yan Hui: "Perhaps he was almost there!"

sentences.zh: 」殆，近也。

translations.literal: " Dai means near.
translations.idiomatic: " Here dai means "close to" or "nearly.""

sentences.zh: 徐囁字孺子，豫章南昌人也。

translations.literal: Xu Nie, courtesy name Ruzi, was a man of Nanchang in Yuzhang (the name graph is often read Zhi in other editions).
translations.idiomatic: Xu Zhi, styled Ruzi, came from Nanchang in Yuzhang commandery (the received text writes his personal name with an alternate graph).

sentences.zh: [一]家貧，常自耕稼，非其力不食。

translations.literal: [One] His household was poor; he always personally ploughed and sowed; what was not by his strength he did not eat.
translations.idiomatic: [One] His family was poor, so he tilled the fields himself and would eat nothing he had not earned by his own labor.

sentences.zh: 恭儉義讓，所居服其德。

translations.literal: Respectful, frugal, righteous, yielding—wherever he dwelt they submitted to his virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Courtesy, frugality, justice, and modesty marked him everywhere he lived, and the people honored his moral power.

sentences.zh: 屢辟公府，不起。

translations.literal: Repeatedly summoned to the Duke's office, he did not rise.
translations.idiomatic: The high ministers summoned him again and again, yet he never took office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]豫章，郡，今洪州也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Yuzhang is a commandery; today it is Hong prefecture.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Yuzhang commandery corresponds roughly to modern Nanchang, Jiangxi.

sentences.zh: 南昌，縣，即今豫章縣也。

translations.literal: Nanchang is a county; that is today's Yuzhang county.
translations.idiomatic: Nanchang was the county seat; in later ages the name Yuzhang was applied to the same place.

sentences.zh: 謝承書曰「稚少為諸生，學嚴氏春秋、京氏易、歐陽尚書，兼綜風角、星官、筭歷、河圖、七緯、推步、變易，異行矯時俗，閭裡服其德化。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book says: "In youth Zhi was a student; he studied the Yan clan Spring and Autumn, the Jing clan Changes, the Ouyang Documents, and also combined wind-horns, star officials, calendrical reckoning, River Chart, seven wefts, step-pushing, and change divination; his unusual conduct corrected the vulgar customs of the time; hamlets and lanes submitted to his virtue and transformation."
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng records that as a young scholar Xu Zhi mastered the Yan Gongyang tradition, the Jing Fang Book of Changes, and the Ouyang Shangshu, while also studying astrology, calendrics, apocrypha, and prognostication; his conduct rebuked shallow fashion until every lane in his district yielded to his moral influence.

sentences.zh: 有失物者，縣以相還，道無拾遺。

translations.literal: When someone lost a thing, the county thereby returned it to each other; on the roads there was none who picked up lost articles.
translations.idiomatic: Lost property was restored through the magistrate's office, and no one pocketed what others dropped in the street.

sentences.zh: 四察孝廉，五辟宰府，三舉茂才」也。

translations.literal: Four times investigated Filial and Incorrupt, five times summoned to the minister's office, three times raised Outstanding Talent.""
translations.idiomatic: He was nominated four times as filial and incorrupt, summoned five times to ministerial offices, and thrice recommended as outstanding talent."

sentences.zh: 時陳蕃為太守，以禮請署功曹，囁不免之，既謁而退。

translations.literal: At the time Chen Fan was prefect; with ritual he requested him to take acting merit clerk; Nie could not avoid it; after paying court he withdrew.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen Fan became prefect he courteously asked Xu Zhi to serve as acting merit assessor; Xu could not refuse outright, but after one formal visit he resigned the post.

sentences.zh: 蕃在郡不接賓客，唯囁來特設一榻，去則縣之。

translations.literal: Fan in the commandery did not receive guests; only when Nie came did he specially set out one couch; when he left then he hung it up.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Fan received no visitors while in office except Xu Zhi, for whom he reserved a couch that he hung from the rafters whenever Xu left.

sentences.zh: 後舉有道，家拜太原太守，[一]皆不就。

translations.literal: Later he was raised as having the Way; at home they invested him as Prefect of Taiyuan; [one] he all did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Later he was recommended as a man of the Way and offered appointment as prefect of Taiyuan without coming to the capital; [one] he refused every post.

sentences.zh: 注[一]就家而拜之也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: It means they went to his home and invested him.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The court sent the commission to his house rather than summoning him to audience.

sentences.zh: 延熹二年，尚書令陳蕃、僕射胡廣等上疏薦囁等曰：「臣聞善人天地之紀，政之所由也。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yanxi, Director of the Secretariat Chen Fan, Vice Director Hu Guang and others submitted a memorial recommending Nie and others, saying: "Your subject has heard that good men are the cords of Heaven and Earth, that by which government proceeds.
translations.idiomatic: In 159 CE Director Chen Fan and Vice Director Hu Guang jointly memorialized: "We have heard that good men are the binding thread of heaven and earth and the root of sound government."

sentences.zh: [一]詩云：『思皇多士，生此王國。

translations.literal: [One] The Odes says: 'Long may Heaven's many officers be born in this royal state.
translations.idiomatic: [One] The Classic of Odes says, "Heaven sends down its host of talents to be born in the king's domain."

sentences.zh: 』[二]天挺俊乂，為陛下出，當輔弼明時，左右大業者也。

translations.literal: ' [Two] Heaven raises outstanding heroes, who come forth for Your Majesty; they ought to assist and brighten the enlightened age, aiding left and right the great enterprise.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Heaven raises eminent men on Your Majesty's behalf to steady a brilliant reign and uphold the great work of state."

sentences.zh: [三]伏見處士豫章徐囁、彭城姜肱、汝南袁閎、[四] 京兆韋著、[五]穎川李曇，德行純備，著於人聽。

translations.literal: [Three] Your subject humbly sees recluses Xu Nie of Yuzhang, Jiang Gong of Pengcheng, Yuan Hong of Runan, [four] Wei Zhu of Jingzhao, [five] Li Tan of Yingchuan—their conduct and virtue are pure and complete, manifest in what men hear.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] We note the recluses Xu Zhi of Yuzhang, Jiang Gong of Pengcheng, Yuan Hong of Runan, [four] Wei Zhu of the capital region, [five] and Li Tan of Yingchuan—men of flawless reputation whose names are on every tongue.

sentences.zh: 若使擢登三事，協亮天工，必能翼宣盛美，增光日明矣。

translations.literal: If you cause them to be raised to the Three Offices, harmonize and brighten Heaven's work, they will surely be able to wing-spread and proclaim flourishing beauty, adding lustre to the sun's brightness.
translations.idiomatic: If they were promoted to the three highest posts to help harmonize the work of heaven, they would spread the dynasty's glory and add luster to the throne."

sentences.zh: 」桓帝乃以安車玄纁，備禮征之，並不至。

translations.literal: " Emperor Huan then with secure carriage and dark-red silk, complete ritual, summoned them; together they did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: " Emperor Huan therefore dispatched the full ritual of cushioned carriage and silks to summon them all, yet none would come.

sentences.zh: 帝因問蕃曰：「徐囁、袁閎、韋著誰為先後？」

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon asked Fan, saying: "Xu Nie, Yuan Hong, Wei Zhu—who comes first and who after?""
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then asked Chen Fan which of Xu Zhi, Yuan Hong, and Wei Zhu deserved first rank."

sentences.zh: 蕃對曰：「閎出生公族，聞道漸訓。

translations.literal: Fan replied, saying: "Hong was born from a ducal clan; hearing the Way he was gradually trained.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Fan answered: "Yuan Hong was born into a great house and absorbed the Way through steady instruction.

sentences.zh: 著長於三輔禮義之俗，所謂不扶自直，不鏤自雕。

translations.literal: Zhu grew up in the custom of ritual and duty in the Three Surrounds; what is called not propped yet straight itself, not carved yet engraved itself.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Zhu grew up amid the ritual ethos of the capital region—like hemp that grows straight without a stake, like jade that needs no chasing.

sentences.zh: [六]至於囁者，爰自江南卑薄之域，而角立傑出，宜當為先。

translations.literal: [Six] As for Nie, from the beginning from the mean and thin region south of the river, yet horn-standing outstanding and eminent, fittingly ought to be first.
translations.idiomatic: [Six] Xu Zhi, though he rose from the humble southland, stands like a horn above the herd and should take first place."

sentences.zh: 」[七] 注[一]左傳曰，晉三□害伯宗，譖而殺之，及欒弗忌。

translations.literal: " [Seven] Commentary [one]: The Zuo Tradition says: Jin's three [lacuna] harmed Bozong, slandered and killed him, reaching Luan Fuji's case.
translations.idiomatic: " [Seven] Note one: The Zuo Tradition tells how certain Jin ministers destroyed the loyal Bozong (the text is damaged).

sentences.zh: 韓獻子曰「□氏其不免乎！

translations.literal: Han Xianzi said: "The [lacuna] clan—will they not fail to escape!
translations.idiomatic: Han Jue lamented that the clan would not escape ruin.

sentences.zh: 善人天地之紀也，而驟絕之，不亡何待」也。

translations.literal: Good men are the cords of Heaven and Earth, yet abruptly to cut them off—what but destruction awaits!"
translations.idiomatic: "Good men are heaven's own cord," he said, "and to cut them off is to invite doom.""

sentences.zh: 注[二]大雅文王之詩也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: It is a poem of Greater Elegant King Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The quotation comes from the "King Wen" ode in the Greater Elegant section.

sentences.zh: 思，願也。

translations.literal: Si means to long.
translations.idiomatic: Here si means "to long for" or "to pray."

sentences.zh: 皇，天也。

translations.literal: Huang means Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Huang in the ode stands for Heaven.

sentences.zh: 思願天多生賢人於此王國。

translations.literal: Longing prays Heaven to many bear worthy men in this royal state.
translations.idiomatic: The line prays that Heaven will send many worthy men to serve this royal house.

sentences.zh: 注[三]左右，助也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Left and right means to assist.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: "Left and right" means to support or assist the ruler.

sentences.zh: 注[四]閎見袁安傳。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Hong is seen in the biography of Yuan An.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: Yuan Hong's life is treated in the biography of Yuan An.

sentences.zh: 謝承書曰：「閎少修志節，矯俗高厲。」

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book says: "In youth Hong cultivated purpose and integrity, correcting the vulgar and lofty and stern.""
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng records that Yuan Hong in youth tempered his will, scorned vulgar fashion, and held himself to a severe standard."

sentences.zh: 注[五]著見韋彪傳。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Zhu is seen in the biography of Wei Biao.
translations.idiomatic: Note five: Wei Zhu appears in the biography of Wei Biao.

sentences.zh: 謝承書曰：「為三輔冠族。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book says: "They were a cap-clan of the Three Surrounds.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng calls the Weis a leading clan of the capital region.

sentences.zh: 著少修節操，持京氏易、韓詩，博通術蓺。」

translations.literal: In youth Zhu cultivated integrity, grasped the Jing clan Changes and Han Odes, broadly penetrated arts and skills.""
translations.idiomatic: Wei Zhu in youth cultivated integrity, mastered the Jing Fang Changes and the Han version of the Odes, and was learned in technical lore."

sentences.zh: 注[六]說苑曰「蓬生枲中，不扶自直」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [six]: The Garden of Persuasions says: "Dock grows amid hemp; without propping it is straight itself."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Note six: Liu Xiang's Garden of Persuasions compares such men to dock growing among hemp, straight without a stake."

sentences.zh: 注[七]如角之特立也。

translations.literal: Commentary [seven]: Like a horn standing alone.
translations.idiomatic: Note seven: The image is of a single horn thrusting up from the herd.

sentences.zh: 囁嘗為太尉黃瓊所辟，不就。

translations.literal: Nie was once summoned by Grand Commandant Huang Qiong; he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Zhi was once summoned by Grand Commandant Huang Qiong but declined to serve.

sentences.zh: 及瓊卒歸葬，囁乃負糧徒步到江夏赴之，設雞酒薄祭，哭畢而去，不告姓名。

translations.literal: When Qiong died and was returned for burial, Nie then shouldered grain, went on foot to Jiangxia to attend it, set out chicken and wine for a thin offering, wept finished and departed, not telling name and surname.
translations.idiomatic: When Huang Qiong died and his body was taken home for burial, Xu Zhi shouldered provisions and walked all the way to Jiangxia, laid a simple offering of chicken and wine, wept his fill, and left without giving his name.

sentences.zh: [一]時會者四方名士郭林宗等數十人，聞之，疑其囁也，乃選能言語生茅容輕騎追之。

translations.literal: [One] At the time those assembled were several tens of famous gentlemen of the four quarters such as Guo Linzong; hearing of it, they suspected it was Nie; then they selected the eloquent student Mao Rong on light horse to pursue him.
translations.idiomatic: [One] Several dozen eminent guests including Guo Tai were present; guessing the mourner had been Xu Zhi, they sent the articulate student Mao Rong after him on a fast horse.

sentences.zh: 及於塗，容為設飯，共言稼穡之事。

translations.literal: When they reached on the road, Rong for him set out a meal; together they spoke of sowing and reaping matters.
translations.idiomatic: Mao overtook him on the road, offered a meal, and they talked only of farming.

sentences.zh: 臨訣去，謂容曰：「為我謝郭林宗，大樹將顛，非一繩所維，何為棲棲不遑寧處？」

translations.literal: At parting leaving, he said to Rong: "For me decline to Guo Linzong: the great tree is about to fall; it is not one rope that can tie it—why be restless and not obtain peaceful dwelling?""
translations.idiomatic: As he took his leave he told Mao, "Give Guo Tai my thanks: when a great tree is about to fall, a single cord cannot hold it—why should he bustle about without a moment's rest?""

sentences.zh: [二]及林宗有母憂，囁往吊之，置生芻一束於廬前而去。

translations.literal: [Two] When Linzong had mourning for his mother, Nie went to condole; he placed one bundle of fresh fodder before the hut and departed.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] When Guo Tai was mourning his mother, Xu Zhi came to offer condolences, laid a bundle of fresh hay before the mourning shed as his gift, and vanished.

sentences.zh: 觿怪，不知其故。

translations.literal: Xi wondered, not knowing its reason.
translations.idiomatic: Those present wondered at it and could not guess the meaning (the subject graph may be corrupt in the edition).

sentences.zh: 林宗曰：「此必南州高士徐孺子也。

translations.literal: Linzong said: "This must be the lofty gentleman of the southern province, Xu Ruzi.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Tai said, "That can only be Xu Ruzi, the great recluse of the south."

sentences.zh: 詩不雲乎，『生芻一束，其人如玉。

translations.literal: Does not the Odes say: 'One bundle of fresh fodder—that man is like jade.
translations.idiomatic: Does not the Odes say, "A bundle of fresh grass—the man is like polished jade"?"

sentences.zh: 』[三]吾無德以堪之。

translations.literal: ' [Three] I lack virtue sufficient to bear it.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] I lack the virtue to deserve such a tribute."

sentences.zh: 」 注[一]謝承書曰：「囁諸公所辟雖不就，有死喪負笈赴吊。

translations.literal: " Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book says: "Although the various lords' summonings to Nie he did not accept, when there was death and mourning he shouldered his satchel and went to condole.
translations.idiomatic: " Note one: Xie Cheng records that though Xu Zhi refused every summons, he would shoulder his book-basket and travel to any funeral.

sentences.zh: 常於家豫炙雞一隻，以一兩撓絮漬酒中，暴干以裡雞，逕到所起頤□外，以水漬撓使有酒氣，斗米飯，白茅為藉，以雞置前，醊酒畢，留謁則去，不見喪主。」

translations.literal: He constantly at home beforehand roasted one chicken, took one liang of twisted floss steeped in wine, sun-dried it to wrap the chicken, went straight to the place where the mourning shed was raised [lacuna] outside, with water steeped the twist so it had wine smell, a peck of rice, white rush grass as mat, placed the chicken in front, when the libation wine was finished, if he left a calling card then departed, not seeing the mourning master.""
translations.idiomatic: At home he would roast a chicken in advance, steep a small bundle of floss in wine, dry it, and wrap the bird; he carried it to the mourning site, sprinkled the floss with water so the wine scent lingered, set out plain rice on white rushes with the chicken before it, poured the libation, left his card, and left without meeting the bereaved."

sentences.zh: 注[二]顛，僕也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Dian means to fall.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: "Fall" here means to topple, as a tree.

sentences.zh: 維，系也。

translations.literal: Wei means to tie.
translations.idiomatic: Wei means to bind or secure with a rope.

sentences.zh: 喻時將衰季，豈一人可能救邪？

translations.literal: It analogizes the time about to be declining season—can one man possibly save it?
translations.idiomatic: The image is of a failing age that no single man can prop up.

sentences.zh: 注[三]小雅白駒詩。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: An ode of Lesser Elegant White Colt.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: The lines come from "White Colt" in the Lesser Elegant.

sentences.zh: 此戒賢者，行所捨，主人之餼雖薄，要就賢主人，其德如玉然也。

translations.literal: This warns the worthy man: in the lodging where he travels, though the host's gift is thin, the essential is to draw near to a worthy host; his virtue is like jade thus.
translations.idiomatic: The ode counsels the worthy guest to seek out a worthy host even when the meal is humble, for such a host's virtue is like jade.

sentences.zh: 靈帝初，欲蒲輪聘囁，會卒，時年七十二。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Ling, they wished with rush wheels to engage Nie; he happened to die; at the time his age was seventy-two sui.
translations.idiomatic: Early in Emperor Ling's reign the court prepared to summon him with the cushioned wheel carriage reserved for the venerable, but he died first, aged seventy-two.

sentences.zh: 子胤字季登，篤行孝悌，亦隱居不仕。

translations.literal: His son Yin, courtesy name Jideng, earnestly practiced filiality and fraternal duty; also he hid and dwelt without serving.
translations.idiomatic: His son Xu Yin, styled Jideng, was deeply filial and brotherly and likewise lived in seclusion without taking office.

sentences.zh: [一]太守華歆禮請相見，固病不詣。

translations.literal: [One] Prefect Hua Xin with ritual requested to meet him; firmly claiming illness he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: [One] Prefect Hua Xin courteously asked to meet him, but he pleaded illness and stayed away.

sentences.zh: [二] 漢末寇賊從橫，皆敬胤禮行，轉相約□，不犯其閭。

translations.literal: [Two] At the end of Han bandits and robbers ran rampant; all respected Yin's ritual conduct, mutually passed agreement [lacuna], not violating his lanes.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] In the last years of Han, when bandits swarmed the countryside, they respected Xu Yin's virtue and pledged among themselves not to enter his lane.

sentences.zh: 建安中卒。

translations.literal: He died in the Jian'an period.
translations.idiomatic: He died during the Jian'an era.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰「胤少遭父母喪，致哀毀瘁，歐血發病。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book says: "In youth Yin encountered the death of father and mother; he brought grief to emaciation, vomited blood and fell ill.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Xie Cheng records that Xu Yin lost both parents while young, mourned until he wasted away and spat blood.

sentences.zh: 服闋，隱居林藪，躬耕稼穡，□則誦經，貧窶困乏，執志彌固，不受惠於人」也。

translations.literal: When mourning ended, he hid and dwelt in woods and marshes, personally ploughed sowing and reaping; [lacuna] then chanted the classics; poor and exhausted, holding his purpose ever firmer, he did not receive favor from others."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: After the mourning term he withdrew to the woods, tilled his own fields, recited the classics by night, endured bitter want, clung to his resolve, and accepted charity from no one."

sentences.zh: 注[二]魏志曰，歆字子魚，平原人。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Wei Records says: Xin, courtesy name Ziyu, was a man of Pingyuan.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Records of Wei give Hua Xin's style as Ziyu and his home as Pingyuan.

sentences.zh: 為豫章太守。

translations.literal: He became Prefect of Yuzhang.
translations.idiomatic: He served as prefect of Yuzhang.

sentences.zh: 為政清淨不煩，吏人鹹感而愛之。

translations.literal: In governing he was clear and quiet, not troublesome; clerks and people all were moved and loved him.
translations.idiomatic: His administration was spare and unruffled, and officials and commoners alike admired and loved him.

sentences.zh: 李曇字雲，少孤，繼母嚴酷，曇事之愈謹，[一]為鄉里所稱法。

translations.literal: Li Tan, courtesy name Yun, in youth was orphaned; his stepmother was harsh and cruel; Tan served her ever more carefully; [one] he was by village and community taken as model and law.
translations.idiomatic: Li Tan, styled Yun, lost his father young and faced a harsh stepmother, yet he served her with growing care until the whole district held him up as a pattern.

sentences.zh: 養親行道，終身不仕。

translations.literal: Nurturing kin and walking the Way, his whole life he did not serve.
translations.idiomatic: He supported his parents, lived by the Way, and never entered office.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「曇少喪父，躬事繼母。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book says: "In youth Tan lost his father; personally he served his stepmother.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Xie Cheng records that Li Tan lost his father while young and waited on his stepmother himself.

sentences.zh: *[繼母]*酷烈，曇性純孝，定省恪勤，妻子恭奉，寒苦執勞，不以為怨。

translations.literal: *[Stepmother]* fierce and harsh; Tan's nature was pure filiality; attending morning and evening respectfully diligent; wife and children respectfully served; in cold and hardship held toil; he did not take it as resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Though his stepmother was cruel, his filial devotion never wavered: he attended her morning and night, kept wife and children in reverent support, and bore poverty and drudgery without a word of complaint.

sentences.zh: 得四時珍玩，先以進母。

translations.literal: Obtaining seasonal rare curiosities, first he presented them to his mother.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever rare delicacies came his way, he sent them first to his mother.

sentences.zh: 與徐孺子等海內列名五處士焉。」

translations.literal: With Xu Ruzi and others within the seas he was listed by name among the five recluses.""
translations.idiomatic: He was reckoned with Xu Zhi among the five famous recluses of the age."

sentences.zh: 姜肱字伯淮，彭城廣戚人也。

translations.literal: Jiang Gong, courtesy name Bohuai, was a man of Guangqi in Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Gong, styled Bohuai, was a native of Guangqi in Pengcheng commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]家世名族。

translations.literal: [One] For generations a famous clan.
translations.idiomatic: [One] His family had long been eminent.

sentences.zh: [二]肱與二弟仲海、季江，俱以孝行著聞。

translations.literal: [Two] Gong together with two younger brothers Zhonghai and Jijiang, all by filial conduct were renowned and heard.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Jiang Gong and his brothers Zhonghai and Jijiang were all celebrated for filial devotion.

sentences.zh: 其友愛天至，常共臥起。

translations.literal: Their friendly love was heaven-sent; they constantly together slept and rose.
translations.idiomatic: Natural affection bound them, and they shared the same bed and daily routine.

sentences.zh: [三]及各娶妻，兄弟相戀，不能別寑，以系嗣當立，乃遞往就室。

translations.literal: [Three] When each married a wife, the brothers longed for one another, could not separate sleeping quarters; because heirs ought to be established, then in turn they went to enter the chamber.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] Even after each married they could not bear to sleep apart until duty to produce heirs forced them to take turns visiting their wives' rooms.

sentences.zh: 注[一]廣戚故城今徐州沛縣東。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The old walled city of Guangqi is east of Pei county in Xu prefecture today.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The old site of Guangqi lies east of present Pei County in Jiangsu.

sentences.zh: 注[二]謝承書曰「祖父豫章太守，父任城相」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Xie Cheng's book says: "The grandfather was Prefect of Yuzhang; the father was chancellor of Rencheng."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: Xie Cheng records a grandfather who was prefect of Yuzhang and a father who was chancellor of Rencheng principality."

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰「肱性篤孝，事繼母恪勤。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Xie Cheng's book says: "Gong's nature was deeply filial; serving his stepmother he was respectful and diligent.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: Xie Cheng says Jiang Gong was deeply filial and served his stepmother with exacting care.

sentences.zh: 母既年少，又嚴厲。
