translations.literal: The mother was moreover young, and also stern and harsh.
translations.idiomatic: The woman was still young and very strict.

sentences.zh: 肱感愷風之孝，兄弟同被而寑，不入房室，以慰母心」也。

translations.literal: Gong was moved by the filiality of the gentle wind's ode; brothers shared one coverlet and slept, did not enter inner chambers, in order to console the mother's heart."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Recalling the brothers in the Odes who shared a quilt, they slept together and avoided the inner rooms to reassure their stepmother."

sentences.zh: 肱博通五經，兼明星緯，士之遠來就學者三千餘人。

translations.literal: Gong broadly penetrated the Five Classics, and also understood stars and wefts; gentlemen who came from afar to study were more than three thousand.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Gong mastered the Five Classics and astrology, and drew more than three thousand students from distant parts.

sentences.zh: 諸公爭加辟命，皆不就。

translations.literal: The various lords vied to add summonses; all he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: High ministers competed to employ him, but he turned down every offer.

sentences.zh: 二弟名聲相次，亦不應徵聘，時人慕之。

translations.literal: The two younger brothers' fame and voice succeeded one another; they also did not answer summons and engagement; men of the time admired them.
translations.idiomatic: His brothers' reputations ran close behind his own, and they too refused every summons, to the admiration of the age.

sentences.zh: 肱嘗與季江謁郡，夜於道遇盜，欲殺之。

translations.literal: Gong once together with Jijiang paid court to the commandery; at night on the road they encountered bandits who wished to kill them.
translations.idiomatic: Once Jiang Gong and his brother Jijiang were travelling to the commandery seat when night fell and bandits ambushed them, meaning to kill them both.

sentences.zh: 肱兄弟更相爭死，賊遂兩釋焉，[一] 但掠奪衣資而已。

translations.literal: The Gong brothers alternately vied to die; the bandits thereupon released both; [one] only plundered clothes and goods, that was all.
translations.idiomatic: [One] Each brother begged to die in the other's place, so moved were the robbers that they let them both go, taking only their clothing and money.

sentences.zh: 既至郡中，見肱無衣服，怪問其故，肱托以它辭，終不言盜。

translations.literal: When they had reached the commandery, seeing Gong without clothes, they wondered and asked the reason; Gong pleaded with other words, in the end did not speak of the bandits.
translations.idiomatic: When they reached the city half naked, officials pressed for an explanation; Jiang invented another excuse and never mentioned the robbery.

sentences.zh: 盜聞而感悔，後乃就精廬，[二]求見征君。

translations.literal: The bandits heard and were moved and repented; afterward then they went to his study hut, [two] sought audience with the summoned gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: When the robbers heard of his silence they were ashamed, sought him out at his rustic hall, [two] and begged audience with the gentleman who had spurned the court.

sentences.zh: 肱與相見，皆叩頭謝罪，而還所略物。

translations.literal: Gong met with them; all knocked head apologizing for guilt, and returned what they had plundered.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang received them; they kowtowed in apology and returned what they had stolen.

sentences.zh: 肱不受，勞以酒食而遣之。

translations.literal: Gong did not accept; he consoled them with wine and food and sent them off.
translations.idiomatic: He refused the goods, fed them, and sent them away in peace.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰「肱與季江俱乘車行適野廬，為賊所劫，取其衣物，欲殺其兄弟。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book says: "Gong together with Jijiang both rode in a carriage travelling to a field hut; they were seized by bandits who took their clothes and goods and wished to kill the brothers.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Xie Cheng tells how the brothers were riding to a country retreat when bandits stripped them and prepared to kill them.

sentences.zh: 肱謂盜曰：『弟年幼，父母所憐愍，又未娉娶，願自殺身濟弟。

translations.literal: Gong said to the bandits: 'The younger brother is young; father and mother pity him; moreover he has not yet betrothed and married; I wish myself to kill my body to save the younger brother.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Gong told them, "My brother is young, still our parents' darling, and not yet married—take my life and spare his."

sentences.zh: 』季江言：

translations.literal: ' Jijiang spoke:
translations.idiomatic: " Jijiang cried,

sentences.zh: 『兄年德在前，家之珍寶，國之英俊，乞自受戮，以代兄命。

translations.literal: ' The elder brother's years and virtue come first; he is the family's treasure, the state's outstanding hero; I beg myself to receive execution in place of the elder brother's life.
translations.idiomatic: "My brother is older, worthier, the jewel of our house and a pillar of the state—let me die in his stead."

sentences.zh: 』盜戢刃曰：『二君所謂賢人，吾等不良，妄相侵犯。

translations.literal: ' The bandits sheathed their blades, saying: 'You two gentlemen are what are called worthy men; we are not good; rashly we offended.
translations.idiomatic: " The robbers put up their weapons and said, "You are true gentlemen and we are worthless curs to have molested you."

sentences.zh: 』□物而去。

translations.literal: ' [lacuna] things and departed.
translations.idiomatic: They left the goods behind and withdrew (the verb is damaged in the received text).

sentences.zh: 肱車中尚有數千錢，盜不見也，使從者追以與之，亦復不受。

translations.literal: In Gong's carriage there still were several thousand cash; the bandits had not seen them; he ordered attendants to pursue and give them to them; again they would not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Several thousand cash remained hidden in the cart, which the robbers had overlooked; Jiang sent servants after them with the money, but they refused it again.

sentences.zh: 肱以物經歷盜手，因以付亭吏而去」也。

translations.literal: Gong because the goods had passed through bandit hands, therefore handed them to the pavilion clerk and departed."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Because the goods had passed through robbers' hands, Jiang gave them to the local post clerk for safekeeping and took his leave."

sentences.zh: 注[二]精廬即精舍也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: A refined hut is the same as a refined dwelling.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: "Refined hut" here means a scholar's retreat or private hall.

sentences.zh: 後與徐囁俱征，不至。

translations.literal: Later together with Xu Nie both were summoned; they did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: Later he and Xu Zhi were summoned together; neither appeared at court.

sentences.zh: 桓帝乃下彭城使畫工圖其形狀。

translations.literal: Emperor Huan then ordered Pengcheng to send a painter to draw his appearance.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan ordered the commandery of Pengcheng to dispatch a painter to record his likeness.

sentences.zh: 肱臥於幽闇，以被韜面，[一]言患眩疾，不欲出風。

translations.literal: Gong lay in dark obscurity, with a quilt covering his face; [one] he said he suffered dizzying illness and did not wish to go out into the wind.
translations.idiomatic: Jiang Gong hid in a dark room with the quilt drawn over his face, [one] claiming vertigo and an aversion to drafts.

sentences.zh: 工竟不得見之。

translations.literal: The artisan in the end could not see him.
translations.idiomatic: The painter never got a look at him.

sentences.zh: 注[一]韜，臧也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Tao means to hide.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: "Cover" here means to conceal or wrap.

sentences.zh: 中常侍曹節等專執朝事，新誅太傅陳蕃﹑大將軍竇武，欲借寵賢德，以釋觿望，乃白征肱為太守。

translations.literal: The palace attendants Cao Jie and others monopolized holding court affairs; they had newly executed Grand Tutor Chen Fan and Grand General Dou Wu; they wished to borrow favor from worthy virtue, in order to release xi hope; then they reported summoning Gong as prefect.
translations.idiomatic: When the eunuchs led by Cao Jie controlled the government, fresh from killing Grand Tutor Chen Fan and General Dou Wu, they sought to court good opinion by appointing worthy men and therefore memorialized an offer to make Jiang Gong a prefect.

sentences.zh: 肱得詔，乃私告其友曰：「吾以虛獲實，遂藉聲價。

translations.literal: When Gong obtained the edict, then he privately told his friends, saying: "I by emptiness obtained substance; thereby I borrow voice and price.
translations.idiomatic: When the edict reached him, Jiang confided to his friends, "Empty praise has made me seem substantial, and now my name is being traded for political credit.

sentences.zh: 明明在上，猶當固其本志，況今政在閹豎，夫何為哉！

translations.literal: Bright brightness is above—still one ought to firm one's original intent; how much more now government is in the castrate slaves—what is to be done!
translations.idiomatic: When the sage kings watched from above, a man might still cling to his first resolve; how much more today, when power sits with the palace slaves—what could I possibly do there?"

sentences.zh: 」乃隱身遯命，遠浮海濱。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he hid his person and fled his fate, floating far to the sea shore.
translations.idiomatic: " He vanished from sight, fled the summons, and took refuge far off on the coast.

sentences.zh: 再以玄纁聘，不就。

translations.literal: Again with dark-red silk they engaged him; he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: They sent a second invitation with the black-and-crimson silks; he still refused.

sentences.zh: 即拜太中大夫，詔書至門，[一]肱使家人對云「久病就醫」。

translations.literal: Immediately he was appointed Grand Palace Counselor; when the edict reached the gate, [one] Gong had family members reply saying "long ill seeking a doctor.""
translations.idiomatic: The court then named him grand palace counselor; [one] when the messenger reached his gate, his household answered that he had long been ill and was away seeking treatment."

sentences.zh: 遂羸服閒行，竄伏青州界中，賣卜給食。

translations.literal: Thereupon in worn clothes he travelled stealthily, fled and hid within Qingzhou's borders, selling divinations to supply food.
translations.idiomatic: He slipped away in ragged clothes, hid in Qingzhou, and earned his meals by telling fortunes.

sentences.zh: 召命得斷，家亦不知其處，歷年乃還。

translations.literal: The summons order was able to be cut off; the household also did not know his whereabouts; after years then he returned.
translations.idiomatic: The summons lapsed, his family had no idea where he was, and only after years did he come home.

sentences.zh: 年七十七，熹平二年終於家。

translations.literal: At seventy-seven sui, in the second year of Xiping he ended at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home in 173 CE, aged seventy-seven.

sentences.zh: 弟子陳留劉操追慕肱德，共刊石頌之。

translations.literal: His disciple Liu Cao of Chenliu pursued admiring Gong's virtue; together they carved stone to praise him.
translations.idiomatic: His pupil Liu Cao of Chenliu, who revered Jiang Gong's virtue, joined others in carving an inscription in his honor.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰：「靈帝手筆下詔曰：『肱抗陵雲之志，養浩然之氣，以朕德薄，未肯降志。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book says: "Emperor Ling with his own hand wrote down an edict saying: 'Gong resists the intent that scales the clouds, nurtures vast flood qi; because Our virtue is thin, he is not willing to lower his intent.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Xie Cheng records Emperor Ling's autograph edict: "Jiang Gong cherishes a spirit that rises above the clouds and the flood-like breath of moral power; he deems my virtue too slight to bend his will."

sentences.zh: 昔許由不屈，王道為化；

translations.literal: In the past Xu You did not yield, and the kingly Way was thereby transformed;
translations.idiomatic: When Xu You refused the throne, the royal way was perfected through his example;

sentences.zh: 夷﹑齊不撓，周德不虧。

translations.literal: Bo Yi and Shu Qi did not bend, and Zhou's virtue was not diminished.
translations.idiomatic: when Boyi and Shuqi would not compromise, the virtue of Zhou remained intact.

sentences.zh: 州郡以禮優順，勿失其意。』」

translations.literal: Let commanderies and prefectures with ritual treat them generously and compliantly; do not lose their intent.'""
translations.idiomatic: Commanderies and prefectures shall treat such men with courtesy and respect and not thwart their wishes.'""

sentences.zh: 申屠蟠

translations.literal: Shentu Pan.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Pan

sentences.zh: 申屠蟠字子龍，陳留外黃人也。

translations.literal: Shentu Pan, courtesy name Zilong, was a man of Waihuang in Chenliu.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Pan, styled Zilong, was a native of Waihuang in Chenliu commandery.

sentences.zh: 九歲喪父，哀毀過禮。

translations.literal: At nine sui he lost his father; grief destroyed him beyond ritual.
translations.idiomatic: He lost his father at nine and mourned so bitterly that he exceeded every prescribed observance.

sentences.zh: 服除，不進酒肉十餘年。

translations.literal: When mourning was removed, for more than ten years he did not take wine or meat.
translations.idiomatic: For more than a decade after the mourning ended he touched neither wine nor meat.

sentences.zh: 每忌日，輒三日不食。

translations.literal: Each taboo day, then for three days he did not eat.
translations.idiomatic: On each anniversary of his parents' death he fasted for three days.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]海內先賢傳曰：「蟠在頤側致甘露﹑白雉，以孝稱。」

translations.literal: [One] Commentary [one]: The Traditions of Former Worthies Within the Seas says: "At Pan's chin side sweet dew arrived and white pheasants; by filiality he was praised.""
translations.idiomatic: [One] Note one: A collection of worthies records auspicious dew and white pheasants appearing beside him, and celebrates his filial devotion."

sentences.zh: 同郡緱氏女玉為父報讎，[一]殺夫氏之黨，吏執玉以告外黃令梁配，[二]配欲論殺玉。

translations.literal: A woman Yu of the Gou clan of the same commandery avenged her father; [one] she killed her husband's faction's kin; clerks seized Yu and reported to Waihuang Magistrate Liang Pei; [two] Pei wished to sentence Yu to death.
translations.idiomatic: A woman of the Gou family named Yu avenged her father [one] by killing members of her husband's clan; the authorities arrested her and brought her before Magistrate Liang Pei of Waihuang, [two] who prepared to execute her.

sentences.zh: 蟠時年十五，為諸生，進諫曰：「玉之節義，足以感無恥之孫，激忍辱之子。

translations.literal: Pan at the time was fifteen sui, a student; he advanced remonstrance, saying: "Yu's integrity and duty are sufficient to move the shameless grandsons, to stir the sons who endure humiliation.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Pan, fifteen and still a schoolboy, went to the magistrate and said, "Yu Zi's steadfast honor could shame the brazen and rouse every coward who bears an insult in silence.

sentences.zh: 不遭明時，尚當表旌廬墓，況在清聽，而不加哀矜！

translations.literal: Not encountering an enlightened age, still one ought to mark her hut and tomb; how much more under clear hearing, yet not add pity!
translations.idiomatic: Even in a dark age she would deserve a memorial at her gate; how can a just magistrate hear her case and show no mercy?"

sentences.zh: 」配善其言，乃為讞得減死論。

translations.literal: " Pei approved his words; then for her plea obtained a reduced death sentence.
translations.idiomatic: " Liang Pei accepted his argument and reopened the case so that she escaped execution.

sentences.zh: [三]鄉人稱美之。

translations.literal: [Three] Villagers praised him for it.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] The whole district praised him for it.

sentences.zh: 注[一]緱，姓也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Gou is a surname.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Gou is the woman's surname.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢書曰「同縣大女緱玉為從父報仇，殺夫之從母兄李士，姑執玉以告吏」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Continued Han Book says: "An adult daughter Yu of Gou of the same county avenged her father's brother, killed her husband's mother's brother's son Li Shi; the mother-in-law seized Yu and reported to the clerk."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Continued Han History gives fuller particulars: she avenged a paternal uncle by killing a kinsman on her husband's side, and her mother-in-law turned her in."

sentences.zh: 注[三]讞，請也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Yan means to request.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: Here "plea" means a formal petition for reconsideration.

sentences.zh: 家貧，傭為漆工。

translations.literal: His household was poor; he hired out as a lacquer worker.
translations.idiomatic: His family was poor, so he hired himself out as a lacquerer.

sentences.zh: 郭林宗見而奇之。

translations.literal: Guo Linzong saw him and marveled at him.
translations.idiomatic: Guo Tai spotted him and knew he was extraordinary.

sentences.zh: 同郡蔡邕深重蟠，及被州辟，乃辭讓之曰：

translations.literal: Cai Yong of the same commandery deeply valued Pan; when he was summoned by the province, then he declined and yielded, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong of the same commandery held Shentu Pan in the highest esteem; when the province summoned Cai himself, he wrote back declining in these words:

sentences.zh: 「申屠蟠稟氣玄妙，性敏心通，喪親盡禮，幾於毀滅。

translations.literal: " Shentu Pan receives qi dark and subtle; his nature is keen and his mind penetrating; in losing kin he exhausted ritual, nearly to destruction.
translations.idiomatic: " Shentu Pan is endowed with a nature subtle and profound, quick in mind and clear in understanding; he buried his parents with every rite until he nearly destroyed his own health.

sentences.zh: 至行美義，人所鮮能。

translations.literal: Supreme conduct and beautiful duty—what men rarely can do.
translations.idiomatic: His conduct and moral choices are what few men can match.

sentences.zh: 安貧樂潛，味道守真，不為燥濕輕重，[一]不為窮達易節。

translations.literal: Content with poverty, delighting in hiding, tasting the Way and guarding truth; not for dry and wet, light and heavy; [one] not for destitution and success changing integrity.
translations.idiomatic: He loves poverty and seclusion, savors the Way and keeps faith with himself; he is not shifted by fortune or climate, [one] nor does he bend principle for success or failure.

sentences.zh: [二]方之於邕，以齒則長，以德則賢。」

translations.literal: [Two] Compared to Yong, by age then elder, by virtue then worthy.""
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Set beside Yong himself, he is the elder in years and the greater in virtue.""

sentences.zh: 注[一]律歷志曰：「銅為物至精，不為燥濕寒暑變其節，不為風雨暴露改其形，介然有常，似於士君子之行。」

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: The Treatise on Pitch-pipes and Calendar says: "Bronze as a substance is utmost refined; it is not for dry and wet, cold and heat to change its joints; it is not for wind and rain, exposure to alter its form; uprightly it has constancy, like the conduct of scholar gentlemen.""
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The treatise on harmonics compares refined bronze, which neither damp nor heat can warp, to the steadfast conduct of a gentleman."

sentences.zh: 注[二]易曰：「窮則獨善其身，達則兼濟天下。」

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Changes says: "In extremity then alone he cultivates his person; in success then together he aids the world.""
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Book of Changes says that in obscurity a man perfects himself, and in high office he brings benefit to all under heaven."

sentences.zh: 後郡召為主簿，不行。

translations.literal: Later the commandery summoned him as chief clerk; he did not go.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery later called him to serve as chief clerk; he did not answer.

sentences.zh: [一]遂隱居精學，博貫五經，兼明圖緯。

translations.literal: [One] Thereupon he hid and dwelt, refined his learning, broadly penetrated the Five Classics, and also understood charts and wefts.
translations.idiomatic: [One] He withdrew to study deeply, mastered the Five Classics, and became learned in prognosticatory charts and apocrypha.

sentences.zh: 始與濟陰王子居同在太學，子居臨歿，以身托蟠，蟠乃躬推輦車，送喪歸鄉里。

translations.literal: At first together with Wang Ziju of Jiyin he was in the Imperial Academy; when Ziju faced death, he entrusted his person to Pan; Pan then personally pushed the hearse cart, sent the mourning home to the village.
translations.idiomatic: He had studied at the Imperial Academy with Wang Ziju of Jiyin; when Wang was dying he entrusted his funeral to Shentu Pan, who personally pushed the hearse all the way to Wang's home district.

sentences.zh: 遇司隸從事於河鞏之閒，[二]從事義之，為封傳護送，[三]蟠不肯受，投傳於地而去。

translations.literal: He met a Metropolitan Clerk between the River and Gong; [two] the clerk deemed him righteous, for him sealed a transit pass and escorted and sent; [three] Pan was not willing to accept, cast the pass on the ground and departed.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] A metropolitan clerk met him between the Yellow River and Gong county, [three] admired his integrity, and offered an official escort with sealed travel papers; Shentu Pan refused, threw the documents to the ground, and walked on.

sentences.zh: 事畢還學。

translations.literal: When the affair was finished he returned to study.
translations.idiomatic: When the burial was done he returned to the academy.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謝承書曰「蟠前後征辟，文書悉掛於樹，初不顧眄」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Xie Cheng's book says: "Pan before and after summonses and appointments, documents all hung on trees; from the first he did not glance back."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Xie Cheng says that whenever summonses arrived, Shentu Pan hung the documents on a tree and walked away without a backward glance."

sentences.zh: 注[二]百官志曰「司隸從事史十二人，秩百石」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Treatise on Officials says: "Metropolitan clerks twelve persons, rank one hundred shi."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Han bureaucracy lists twelve metropolitan clerks at the hundred-shi rank."

sentences.zh: 注[三]傳謂符牒。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Chuan means talisman documents.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: "Pass" here means the sealed travel warrant.

sentences.zh: 使人監送之。

translations.literal: He sent men to supervise and escort him.
translations.idiomatic: The clerk detailed men to escort him under guard.

sentences.zh: 太尉黃瓊辟，不就。

translations.literal: Grand Commandant Huang Qiong summoned him; he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: Grand Commandant Huang Qiong summoned him, but he would not go.

sentences.zh: 及瓊卒，歸葬江夏，四方名豪會帳下者六七千人，[一]互相談論，莫有及蟠者。

translations.literal: When Qiong died, was returned and buried in Jiangxia, famous heroes of the four quarters who met under the tent were six or seven thousand; [one] they mutually discoursed; none reached Pan.
translations.idiomatic: When Huang Qiong died and his body was taken to Jiangxia for burial, [one] six or seven thousand notables gathered at the mourning site, yet in all their talk none could match Shentu Pan.

sentences.zh: 唯南郡一生與相酬對，既別，執蟠手曰：「君非聘則征，如是相見於上京矣。

translations.literal: Only one student from Nan commandery exchanged answers with him; when they parted, he grasped Pan's hand, saying: "If you are not engaged then summoned, thus we shall meet in the upper capital.
translations.idiomatic: Only one scholar from Nan commandery could hold his own in debate; as they parted the man seized Shentu Pan's hand and said, "Unless you take a private post you will be summoned to court—we shall meet again in the capital."

sentences.zh: 」蟠勃然作色曰：「始吾以子為可與言也，何意乃相拘教樂貴之徒邪？

translations.literal: " Pan suddenly changed color, saying: "At first I thought you were one with whom one could speak—what idea is this, that you bind me to teach the sort who delight in exalted rank?
translations.idiomatic: " Shentu Pan flushed with anger and said, "I thought you worth talking to—why do you lecture me like some climber who lives for rank?"

sentences.zh: 」[二]因振手而去，不復與言。

translations.literal: " [Two] Thereupon he shook off his hand and departed, no longer speaking with him.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] He shook free and strode off without another word."

sentences.zh: 再舉有道，不就。

translations.literal: Again he was raised as having the Way; he did not accept.
translations.idiomatic: He was again recommended as a man of the Way and again stayed home.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]帳下，葬處。

translations.literal: [Three] Commentary [one]: Under the tent means the burial place.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] Note one: "Under the tent" means the site of the funeral gathering.

sentences.zh: 注[二]樂音五孝反。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: For le the reading is wu-xiao fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The word read "delight in" is read in the wu-xiao fan pronunciation.

sentences.zh: 注[三]謝承書曰「詔書令郡以禮發遣，蟠到河南萬歲亭，折轅而旋」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Xie Cheng's book says: "An edict ordered the commandery with ritual to dispatch him; Pan reached the Wansui pavilion in Henan, broke his axle-shaft and returned."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: Xie Cheng adds that when an edict forced the commandery to send him off, Shentu Pan reached the Wansui post station in Henan, snapped his cart axle, and turned back."

sentences.zh: 先是京師游士汝南范滂等非訐朝政，自公卿以下皆折節下之。

translations.literal: Earlier capital wandering scholars such as Fan Pang of Runan and others impeached court government; from dukes and ministers downward all bent their measure and lowered to them.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, wandering scholars at the capital such as Fan Pang of Runan had attacked court policy, and everyone from the highest ministers downward humbled himself before them.

sentences.zh: [一]太學生爭慕其風，以為文學將興，處士復用。

translations.literal: [One] Imperial Academy students vied to admire their wind, thinking letters and learning would rise, recluses would again be employed.
translations.idiomatic: [One] The academy students imitated them in crowds, convinced that learning was about to flourish and recluses would return to power.

sentences.zh: 蟠獨歎曰：「昔戰國之世，處士橫議，[二]列國之王，至為擁篲先驅，[三]卒有坑儒燒書之禍，今之謂矣。

translations.literal: Pan alone sighed, saying: "In the past the Warring States age, recluses ran wild in discussion; [two] the kings of the various states, to the point of holding brooms as vanguard; [three] in the end there was the calamity of burying scholars and burning books—this is what is called today.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Pan alone sighed, "In the Warring States era recluses debated politics without restraint, [two] and kings went so far as to sweep the road before them; [three] it ended in the burying of scholars and the burning of the classics. The same story is unfolding now."

sentences.zh: 」乃絕多於梁碭之閒，[四]因樹為屋，自同傭人。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he broke off many between Liang and Dang; [four] relying on trees he made a hut, himself the same as hired men.
translations.idiomatic: " He broke with the capital set and lived between Liang and Dang, [four] roofed his hut with branches, and worked alongside hired laborers.

sentences.zh: [五]居二年，滂等果罹黨錮，或死或刑者數百人，蟠確然免於疑論。

translations.literal: [Five] Dwelling two years, Pang and others indeed suffered party proscription; those who died or were punished were several hundred persons; Pan solidly was spared from suspicious discourse.
translations.idiomatic: [Five] Within two years Fan Pang and his circle were caught in the faction proscription; hundreds were executed or mutilated, while Shentu Pan alone escaped every suspicion.

sentences.zh: 後蟠友人陳郡馮雍坐事系獄，豫州牧黃琬欲殺之。

translations.literal: Later Pan's friend Feng Yong of Chen commandery sat in affair and was bound in prison; the Governor of Yuzhou Huang Wan wished to kill him.
translations.idiomatic: Later his friend Feng Yong of Chen commandery was jailed on a charge, and Governor Huang Wan of Yuzhou meant to put him to death.

sentences.zh: 或勸蟠救雍，蟠不肯行，曰：「黃子琰為吾故邪，未必合罪。

translations.literal: Someone urged Pan to save Yong; Pan was not willing to go, saying: "Huang Ziyan for my sake? Not necessarily fitting guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Friends urged Shentu Pan to intercede, but he refused, saying, "Huang Wan will not kill Feng Yong on my account, nor is Yong necessarily guilty.

sentences.zh: 如不用吾言，雖往何益！

translations.literal: If he does not use my words, although going what benefit!
translations.idiomatic: If he will not heed me, what good would a visit do?"

sentences.zh: 」琬聞之，遂免雍罪。

translations.literal: " Wan heard it; thereupon he spared Yong's crime.
translations.idiomatic: " When Huang Wan heard this, he dropped the charges against Feng Yong."

sentences.zh: 注[一]訐謂橫議是非也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Jie means to run wild discussing right and wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: "Impeach" here means to attack public policy without restraint.

sentences.zh: 訐或作「評」也。

translations.literal: Jie is sometimes written "ping".
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts write the graph as the one meaning "appraise" instead.

sentences.zh: 注[二]孟子曰：「聖王不作，諸侯恣行，處士橫議。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Mencius says: "When sage kings do not arise, the feudal lords run wild; recluses run wild in discussion.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: Mencius says that when no sage king appears, local lords do as they please and private scholars debate politics recklessly.

sentences.zh: 」前書曰：「秦既稱帝，患周之敗，以為起於處士橫議，諸侯力爭。

translations.literal: " The Former Book says: "When Qin had declared emperor, it worried Zhou's defeat, thought it arose from recluses' wild discussion, feudal lords' forceful contention.
translations.idiomatic: " The History of the Former Han records that the First Emperor blamed the fall of Zhou on unchecked debate among scholars and rivalry among the feudal lords.

sentences.zh: 」音義曰：「言由橫議而敗之。」

translations.literal: " Sounds and Meanings says: "It means by wild discussion they were defeated.""
translations.idiomatic: " A commentary gloss adds that unruly opinion was held to have ruined the state.""

sentences.zh: 注[三]史記，鄒衍如燕，昭王擁篲先驅，請列弟子之坐而受業。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: The Records of the Historian: Zou Yan went to Yan; King Zhao held broom as vanguard, requested to arrange disciple's seat and receive instruction.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: The Records tell how King Zhao of Yan swept the path before Zou Yan and seated him as a master.

sentences.zh: 築碣石宮，身親往師之。

translations.literal: He built Jieshi Palace; personally he went to take him as teacher.
translations.idiomatic: He built the Jieshi Palace and went in person to study under him.

sentences.zh: 注[四]梁國有碭縣。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Liang state had Dang county.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: Dang county lay in the principality of Liang.

sentences.zh: 注[五]謝承書曰「居蓬萊之室，依桑樹以為棟」也。

translations.literal: Commentary [five]: Xie Cheng's book says: "He dwelt in a Penglai chamber, relied on mulberry trees to make rafters."" is the meaning.
translations.idiomatic: Note five: Xie Cheng pictures his hut as a rustic cell with mulberry trunks for rafters."

sentences.zh: 大將軍何進連征不詣，進必欲致之，使蟠同郡黃忠書勸曰：「前莫府初開，至如先生，特加殊禮，優而不名，申以手筆，設幾杖之坐。

translations.literal: Grand General He Jin repeatedly summoned him and he did not come; Jin was determined to bring him, sent Pan's fellow townsman Huang Zhong a letter urging, saying: "Formerly when the General's office first opened, as for a gentleman like you, we specially added extraordinary ritual, honored without naming, extended with own hand writing, set out seat with armrest and staff.
translations.idiomatic: General-in-chief He Jin summoned him repeatedly without success, then sent Shentu Pan's townsman Huang Zhong with a letter: "When my headquarters first opened, I reserved the highest courtesy for you—honoring you without pressing your name, writing in my own hand, setting out a seat with armrest and staff.

sentences.zh: 經過二載，而先生抗志彌高，所尚益固。

translations.literal: Passing two years, yet the gentleman's resisting intent grows higher, what he esteems grows firmer.
translations.idiomatic: Two years have passed, yet your resolve has only grown loftier and your principles harder.

sentences.zh: 竊論先生高節有餘，於時則未也。

translations.literal: Your servant privately judges the gentleman's lofty integrity is more than enough, but for the times then not yet.
translations.idiomatic: I grant that your integrity is more than the age deserves, yet measured against the times it is not enough.

sentences.zh: 今穎川荀爽載病在道，北海鄭玄北面受署。

translations.literal: Now Xun Shuang of Yingchuan rides illness on the road; Zheng Xuan of Beihai faces north receiving appointment.
translations.idiomatic: Xun Shuang of Yingchuan is already on the road though ill, and Zheng Xuan of Beihai has taken his place before me as a subordinate.

sentences.zh: 彼豈樂羈牽哉，知時不可逸豫也。

translations.literal: How could they delight in halter and bridle—they know the time cannot be easy and leisurely.
translations.idiomatic: They do not love the yoke; they know this is no time for ease.

sentences.zh: 昔人之隱，遭時則放聲滅多，巢棲茹薇。

translations.literal: The reclusion of men of old: encountering the times then they released voice and extinguished much; nested in trees and chewed fernbrake (text damaged).
translations.idiomatic: Ancient recluses, when the times turned against them, muffled their voices and hid their traces, built nests in the hills, and lived on wild plants (the received wording is partly corrupt).

sentences.zh: [一]其不遇也，則裸身大笑，被發狂歌。

translations.literal: [One] When they did not meet opportunity, then naked body great laughter, dishevelled hair mad song.
translations.idiomatic: [One] When they failed of recognition they went naked, laughed aloud, let their hair fly, and sang like madmen.

sentences.zh: [二]今先生處平壤，[三] 遊人閒，吟典籍，襲衣裳，事異昔人，而欲遠蹈其多，不亦難乎！

translations.literal: [Two] Now the gentleman dwells on level soil; [three] wanders among men at leisure, chants canons and records, puts on coat and skirt; affairs differ from men of old, yet wishes far to tread their much—is it not difficult!
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Yet you live on open ground, [three] move among ordinary men, chant the classics, and dress like a gentleman; your case is not theirs, yet you would walk their path from afar—how hard that would be!"

sentences.zh: 孔氏可師，何必首陽。

translations.literal: The Kong clan can be taken as teacher—why must it be Shouyang.
translations.idiomatic: Confucius is a worthy model; you need not starve on Mount Shouyang."

sentences.zh: 」[四]蟠不荅。

translations.literal: " [Four] Pan did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] Shentu Pan made no reply."

sentences.zh: 注[一]放，□也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Fang means [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: Note one: The glossed verb is damaged in the text.

sentences.zh: 謂□聲名也。

translations.literal: It means [lacuna] fame and name.
translations.idiomatic: The commentator explains it as hiding one's reputation (the graph is missing).

sentences.zh: 巢棲謂巢父也。

translations.literal: Nest-dwelling means Nest Father.
translations.idiomatic: The allusion to nesting refers to the recluse Chaofu.

sentences.zh: 說文：「薇，似藿也。」

translations.literal: Shuowen: "Wei resembles bean-leaf.""
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen defines the edible fern as a plant like broad beans."

sentences.zh: 注[二]楚詞曰：「桑扈裸行。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Chu ci says: "Sanghu walks naked.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Songs of Chu speak of Sanghu walking naked.

sentences.zh: 」史記曰：「箕子被發陽狂。

translations.literal: " The Records says: "Jizi dishevelled hair feigned madness.
translations.idiomatic: " The Records say Jizi let down his hair and feigned madness.

sentences.zh: 」歌謂楚狂接輿歌而過孔子也。

translations.literal: " Song means the Chu madman Jieyu sang passing Confucius.
translations.idiomatic: " The mad song refers to Jieyu of Chu singing past Confucius."

sentences.zh: 注[三]壤，地也。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Rang means ground.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: "Level soil" means ordinary settled land, not the wilderness.

sentences.zh: 注[四]孔子使子路語隱者云：「不仕無義。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Confucius sent Zilu to speak to the recluse, saying: "Not to serve is without rightness.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: Confucius once sent Zilu to tell a recluse, "To refuse office is to abandon duty."

sentences.zh: 長幼之節，不可廢也；

translations.literal: The gradations of elder and young cannot be cast aside;
translations.idiomatic: The proper order between old and young cannot be discarded;

sentences.zh: 君臣之義如之何其可廢也？

translations.literal: the duty of ruler and minister—how can it be cast aside?"
translations.idiomatic: how then can the bond between ruler and minister be thrown away?"

sentences.zh: 欲潔其身而亂大倫。

translations.literal: Desiring to cleanse one's person yet disordering the great human relationships.
translations.idiomatic: To polish one's private honor while overturning the great human relationships is wrong."

sentences.zh: 」首陽，夷﹑齊所隱山也。

translations.literal: " Shouyang is the mountain where Bo Yi and Shu Qi hid.
translations.idiomatic: " Mount Shouyang is where Boyi and Shuqi chose to starve rather than serve."

sentences.zh: 中平五年，復與爽﹑玄及穎川韓融﹑[一]陳紀等十四人並博士征，不至。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Zhongping, again together with Shuang, Xuan, and Han Rong of Yingchuan, [one] Chen Ji and others fourteen persons together were summoned as Erudites; they did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: In 188 CE he was again named with Xun Shuang, Zheng Xuan, Han Rong of Yingchuan, [one] Chen Ji, and nine others—fourteen men in all—as court academicians; none appeared.

sentences.zh: 明年，董卓廢立，蟠及爽﹑融﹑紀等復俱公車征，[二]唯蟠不到。

translations.literal: The next year, Dong Zhuo deposed and enthroned; Pan together with Shuang, Rong, Ji and others again together were summoned by public carriage; [two] only Pan did not arrive.
translations.idiomatic: The next year, after Dong Zhuo set aside one emperor and raised another, Shentu Pan was summoned again by official carriage along with Xun Shuang, Han Rong, and Chen Ji; [two] Pan alone stayed away.

sentences.zh: 觿人鹹勸之，蟠笑而不應。

translations.literal: Xi people all urged him; Pan smiled but did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: Neighbors and friends urged him to go (the first graph is corrupt in the edition); he only smiled and gave no answer.

sentences.zh: 居無幾，爽等為卓所脅迫，西都長安，京師擾亂。

translations.literal: Dwelling not long, Shuang and others were by Zhuo coerced and pressed, western capital at Chang'an, the capital was disturbed and chaotic.
translations.idiomatic: Soon afterward Xun Shuang and the rest were forced west to Chang'an with Dong Zhuo, and the capital fell into chaos.

sentences.zh: 及大駕西遷，公卿多遇兵饑，室家流散，融等僅以身脫。

translations.literal: When the great chariot moved west, dukes and ministers many met weapons and famine, households scattered and drifted; Rong and others barely by their persons escaped.
translations.idiomatic: When the court fled west, high officials were cut down by soldiers or died of hunger, families were scattered, and Han Rong and his companions barely escaped with their lives.

sentences.zh: 唯蟠處亂末，終全高志。

translations.literal: Only Pan dwelt at the end of chaos, to the end preserving lofty intent.
translations.idiomatic: Shentu Pan alone lived through the tail of the turmoil with his high purpose intact.

sentences.zh: 年七十四，終於家。

translations.literal: At seventy-four sui, he ended at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home at the age of seventy-four.

sentences.zh: 注[一]融字符長，詔之子也。

translations.literal: Commentary [one]: Rong's courtesy name was Bozhang; he was Shao's son.
translations.idiomatic: Note one: Han Rong, styled Bozhang, was the son of Han Shao.

sentences.zh: 見韶傳。

translations.literal: See the biography of Shao.
translations.idiomatic: His father's biography gives further detail.

sentences.zh: 注[二]續漢志曰，征爽為司空，融為尚書，紀為侍中。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: The Continued Han Treatise says: Shuang was summoned as Minister of Works, Rong as Director of the Secretariat, Ji as Palace Attendant.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: The Continued Han records name Xun Shuang minister of works, Han Rong director of the secretariat, and Chen Ji palace attendant for this summons.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：琛寶可懷，貞期難對。

translations.literal: Encomium says: Treasured gems may be cherished; a chaste season is hard to match.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's summation: Moral treasure is worth cherishing, yet a worthy age that answers to it is rare.

sentences.zh: [一]道苟違運，理用同廢。

translations.literal: [One] If the Way chances to oppose the fortune of the age, principle and function alike are cast aside.
translations.idiomatic: [One] When the Way runs counter to the times, both principle and practical use are set at naught.

sentences.zh: 與其遐棲，豈若蒙穢？

translations.literal: Compared with dwelling far in reclusion, how can it match bearing filth?
translations.idiomatic: Is distant reclusion really better than sullying one's hands in a corrupt court?!

sentences.zh: [二]淒淒碩人，陵阿窮退。

translations.literal: [Two] Desolate, desolate the great man; on the hill's bend, utterly withdrawn.
translations.idiomatic: [Two] Desolate stands the great man, withdrawn to the fold of the hills.

sentences.zh: [三]韜伏明姿，甘是堙曖。

translations.literal: [Three] Hiding and crouching bright bearing, willingly this obscuring dimness.
translations.idiomatic: [Three] They hid luminous talent and accepted the gloom of obscurity.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]琛寶喻道德也。

translations.literal: [Four] Commentary [one]: Treasured gems analogize the Way and virtue.
translations.idiomatic: [Four] Note one: "Treasured gems" stands for moral worth.

sentences.zh: 貞期謂明時也。

translations.literal: Chaste season means an enlightened age.
translations.idiomatic: "A chaste season" means an enlightened reign that welcomes worthies.

sentences.zh: 對，偶也。

translations.literal: Dui means pairing.
translations.idiomatic: Dui here means a proper pairing or correspondence.

sentences.zh: 注[二]蒙穢謂仕亂朝。

translations.literal: Commentary [two]: Bearing filth means serving a chaotic court.
translations.idiomatic: Note two: "Bearing filth" means taking office under a disordered government.

sentences.zh: 注[三]碩人謂賢者。

translations.literal: Commentary [three]: Great man means a worthy.
translations.idiomatic: Note three: "Great man" refers to the worthy recluse.

sentences.zh: 淒淒，饑病貌也。

translations.literal: Desolate, desolate is the appearance of hunger and illness.
translations.idiomatic: Desolate here suggests the lean, hungry look of withdrawal.

sentences.zh: 言賢者退而窮處。

translations.literal: It means the worthy withdraws and dwells in extremity.
translations.idiomatic: It describes the worthy man who retires into destitute seclusion.

sentences.zh: 詩國風曰：「考盤在阿，碩人之薖。

translations.literal: The Odes, Airs of the States says: "He fashioned his hut on the slope; the great man is so wide and at ease.
translations.idiomatic: The Airs of the States say, "He has built his hut on the hillside; there the great man dwells at ease."

sentences.zh: 」曲陵曰阿。

translations.literal: " A winding hill is called e.
translations.idiomatic: " E names a curving hillside.

sentences.zh: 陵，升也。

translations.literal: Ling means to ascend.
translations.idiomatic: The word for hill here also carries the sense of rising ground.

sentences.zh: 薖，饑也。

translations.literal: Ke means hunger.
translations.idiomatic: The rare word glossed here suggests hunger or leanness.

sentences.zh: 薖音苦戈反。

translations.literal: For ke the reading is ku-ge fan.
translations.idiomatic: The graph is read ku-ge fan.

sentences.zh: 注[四]堙，沉也。

translations.literal: Commentary [four]: Yin means to sink.
translations.idiomatic: Note four: "Buried" here means sunk or submerged in obscurity.

sentences.zh: 曖猶翳也。

translations.literal: Ai is like shade-veiling.
translations.idiomatic: The second word means shadowed or veiled from sight.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes.
translations.idiomatic: Textual collation notes

sentences.zh: 一七三九頁三行邦無道則可卷而懷也按：「則」字原脫，逕據汲本﹑殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1739 line 3: "When the state lacks the Way then he can roll up and keep in the bosom"—comment: the graph "ze" was originally missing; it was directly supplied according to the Ji edition and Palace edition.
translations.idiomatic: Collation note (p. 1739, line 3): The particle "then" was missing in the base text and has been supplied from the Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一七三九頁九行申徒狄按：汲本﹑殿本「徒」作「屠」。

translations.literal: Page 1739 line 9: Shentu Di—comment: Ji edition and Palace edition write "tu" as "slaughter" tu.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1739, line 9): The Ji and Palace editions write the second syllable of the name with the graph for "butcher" instead.

sentences.zh: 一七四０頁七行謝沉書曰按：汲本﹑殿本「沉」作「承」。

translations.literal: Page 1740 line 7: Xie Chen's book says—comment: Ji edition and Palace edition write "chen" as "receive" cheng.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1740, line 7): The Ji and Palace editions give the historian's name as Xie Cheng, not Xie Chen.

sentences.zh: 一七四０頁一二行仲叔同郡荀恁按：集解引錢大昕說，謂案劉平傳，數薦達名士承宮﹑郇恁等，即此荀恁也。

translations.literal: Page 1740 line 12: Zhongshu same commandery Xun Ren—comment: Collected Explanations cites Qian Daxin's view, saying examining the biography of Liu Ping, repeatedly recommending and advancing famous scholars Cheng Gong, Xun Ren and others—this is that Xun Ren.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1740, line 12): Qian Daxin notes that the Xun Ren mentioned here is the same man recommended in the biography of Liu Ping alongside Cheng Gong.

sentences.zh: 說文無「荀」字，當以「郇」為正。

translations.literal: The Shuowen has no graph "Xun"; one ought to take "Xun" (place name surname) as correct.
translations.idiomatic: The Shuowen does not list the usual surname graph; the original surname form should be preferred.

sentences.zh: 一七四一頁一二行*

translations.literal: Page 1741 line 12: *
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1741, line 12): textual break marker in the edition.

translations.literal: (Also)
translations.idiomatic: Marginal gloss "also" in the apparatus.

sentences.zh: **[多]*若違時據殿本改。

translations.literal: **[many]* if opposing the times—according to Palace edition changed.
translations.idiomatic: The damaged phrase "if opposing the times" has been emended to follow the Palace edition.

sentences.zh: 一七四一頁一四行*

translations.literal: Page 1741 line 14: *
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1741, line 14): asterisk in the source line.

translations.literal: (Law)
translations.idiomatic: The manuscript gloss reads "law" where "judicial" was intended.

sentences.zh: **[決]*曹掾燕之後也據汲本﹑殿本改。

translations.literal: **[decide]* clerk of the bureau—descendant of Yan—according to Ji edition and Palace edition changed.
translations.idiomatic: The line naming the judicial clerk descended from Yan has been corrected from the Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 按：殿本考證雲「決」字監本作「法」。

translations.literal: Comment: Palace edition textual investigation says the graph "decide" in the supervisory edition reads "law".
translations.idiomatic: The Palace edition notes that some copies miswrite "judicial" as "law."

sentences.zh: 王會汾謂周嘉傳言燕於宣帝時為郡決曹掾，則作「法曹」者誤。

translations.literal: Wang Huifen says the biography of Zhou Jia states Yan at Emperor Xuan's time was commandery Judicial Bureau clerk—then writing "Law Bureau" is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Huifen argues from the biography of Zhou Jia that "law bureau" is an error for "judicial bureau."

sentences.zh: 一七四二頁六行常肆勤以自給按：集解引錢大昕說，謂「肆」當為「肄」字之誤。

translations.literal: Page 1742 line 6: constantly spread diligence to supply himself—comment: Collected Explanations cites Qian Daxin, saying "spread" ought to be error for the graph "practice".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1742, line 6): Qian Daxin argues that "spread" is a scribal error for "practice" or "toil."

sentences.zh: 一七四二頁八行擇日翦發為*

translations.literal: Page 1742 line 8: choose day clip hair as *
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1742, line 8): line break after "clip the hair into" in the ritual quotation.

translations.literal: (Tiao)
translations.idiomatic: Marginal reading for the childhood hair graph.

sentences.zh: **[鬌]*據殿本改，與今本禮記合。

translations.literal: **[tuo]* according to Palace edition changed, matching today's Record of Rites.
translations.idiomatic: The following graph has been emended from the Palace edition to match the received Book of Rites.

sentences.zh: 一七四三頁一行遣*[門]*生送敬據刊誤補。

translations.literal: Page 1743 line 1: send *[gate]* student present respects—according to Errata supplement.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1743, line 1): The word "disciple" has been supplied from the errata list ("gate" student).

sentences.zh: 一七四三頁五行□裡先生殿本「□」作「角」。

translations.literal: Page 1743 line 5: [lacuna]-li Master—Palace edition writes [lacuna] as "horn".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1743, line 5): The Palace edition writes Master Luli with the graph for "horn" instead of a damaged character.

sentences.zh: 按：角本有祿音，後人不知，別造「□」字代之。

translations.literal: Comment: Horn originally had lu sound; later men did not know, separately created [lacuna] graph to replace it.
translations.idiomatic: The note explains that later copyists invented a wrong character for a name properly read with the "horn" graph.

sentences.zh: 廣韻一屋亦作「角」，不作「□」。

translations.literal: Guangyun one-house also writes "horn", does not write [lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: The Guang rhyme dictionary lists the form with "horn," not the corrupt graph.

sentences.zh: 一七四三頁一０行良字君郎按：集解引惠棟說，謂袁宏紀「君郎」作「君卿」。

translations.literal: Page 1743 line 10: on Liang's courtesy name Junlang—comment: Collected Explanations cites Hui Dong, saying Yuan Hong's record writes "Junlang" as "Junqing".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1743, line 10): Hui Dong notes that Yuan Hong's history gives Feng Liang's style as Junqing instead of Junlang.

sentences.zh: 一七四四頁二行在慎水之南按：校補謂「南」字疑「陽」字之誤。

translations.literal: Page 1744 line 2: south of the Shen River—comment: Collation supplement says the graph "south" is suspected error for "yang".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1744, line 2): Some editors suspect "south" is a mistake for "yang" in the place name.

sentences.zh: 一七四四頁四行既而前至袁*

translations.literal: Page 1744 line 4: soon after forward arrived Yuan *
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1744, line 4): line break in the Yuan surname passage.

translations.literal: (Hong)
translations.idiomatic: Marginal reading Hong.

sentences.zh: **[閬]*所集解引陳景雲說，謂黃憲﹑袁閬俱慎陽人，故荀淑有「子國顏子」之語，慎陽本侯國也。

translations.literal: **[Lang]* place—Collected Explanations cites Chen Jingyun's view, saying Huang Xian and Yuan Lang both were Shenyang men, therefore Xun Shu had the words "your state's Yan Zi"; Shenyang originally was a marquis state.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Jingyun argues that Huang Xian and Yuan Lang were both from Shenyang, which explains Xun Shu's remark; Shenyang was formerly a marquisate.

sentences.zh: 若汝陽袁閎，與憲同郡異縣，則作「閎」非矣。

translations.literal: If Ruyang Yuan Hong, with Xian same commandery different county, then writing "Hong" is not correct.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong of Ruyang was in a different county from Huang Xian, so the graph Hong would be wrong here.

sentences.zh: 又引黃山說，謂此傳「閎」皆當作「閬」，惟後徐囁傳所載，則確為袁閎耳。

translations.literal: Also cites Huang Shan's view, saying in this biography "Hong" all ought to be written "Lang"; only in the later Xu Nie biography what is recorded is indeed Yuan Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Shan holds that Hong in this chapter should read Lang throughout, except in the later biography of Xu Zhi where Yuan Hong is clearly meant.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now according to this changed.
translations.idiomatic: The text has been emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一七四四頁七行同郡陳蕃周舉按：集解引惠棟說，謂世說及袁宏紀皆作「周子居」。

translations.literal: Page 1744 line 7: same commandery Chen Fan Zhou Ju—comment: Collected Explanations cites Hui Dong's view, saying New Tales of the World and Yuan Hong's record both write "Zhou Ziju".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1744, line 7): Hui Dong notes that other sources give the name as Zhou Ziju rather than Zhou Ju.

sentences.zh: 一七四四頁一０行譬諸*

translations.literal: Page 1744 line 10: compare to *
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1744, line 10): simile breaks in the Guo Tai quotation.

translations.literal: (Fan)
translations.idiomatic: Reading fan for the spring name.

sentences.zh: **[氿]*濫據殿本改。

translations.literal: **[gui]* overflow according to Palace edition changed.
translations.idiomatic: The graph for the spring name has been emended from the Palace edition.

sentences.zh: 注同。

translations.literal: Commentary the same.
translations.idiomatic: The same emendation applies to the commentary.

sentences.zh: 一七四四頁一一行叔度汪汪若千頃陂按：集解引惠棟說，謂「千頃」續漢書作「萬頃」。

translations.literal: Page 1744 line 11: Shudu vast vast like thousand-qing marsh—comment: Collected Explanations cites Hui Dong, saying "thousand qing" in Continued Han Book writes "ten thousand qing".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1744, line 11): Hui Dong notes the Continued Han History reads "ten thousand acres" instead of "thousand."

sentences.zh: 一七四四頁一三行一作閬按：李慈銘謂黃憲傳之「袁閎」，皆為「袁閬」之誤。

translations.literal: Page 1744 line 13: one version writes Lang—comment: Li Ciming says in Huang Xian's biography "Yuan Hong" all are errors for "Yuan Lang".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1744, line 13): Li Ciming argues that Yuan Hong in this chapter should be Yuan Lang throughout.

sentences.zh: 章懷所注者乃是誤本，其云「一作閬」者，乃別據一不誤之本。

translations.literal: What Prince Zhanghuai annotated was a mistaken edition; his saying "one version writes Lang" was then separately based on one not-mistaken edition.
translations.idiomatic: Li adds that Li Xian's commentary followed a faulty text and that his note "alternate reading Lang" came from a sounder manuscript.

sentences.zh: 一七四五頁三行乃彌信宿也按：校補引柳從辰說，謂袁宏紀作「乃彌日信宿也」，多「日」字文義更較圓足。

translations.literal: Page 1745 line 3: then extended two nights—comment: Collation supplement cites Liu Congchen's view, saying Yuan Hong's record writes "then extended days two nights", more "day" graph the sense is more complete.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1745, line 3): Liu Congchen notes that Yuan Hong's text inserts "day" and reads more smoothly.

sentences.zh: 一七四五頁四行奉高閎字也按：李慈銘謂袁閬字奉高，見第五十六卷王龔傳，憲傳與龔傳僅隔兩卷，章懷又見他本之作「閬」，乃不能援以改正，反注奉高為閎字，可謂率謬。

translations.literal: Page 1745 line 4: Fenggao is Hong's courtesy name—comment: Li Ciming says Yuan Lang's courtesy name is Fenggao, seen in scroll 56 Wang Gong biography; Xian's biography and Gong's biography are only two scrolls apart; Prince Zhanghuai again saw other editions' writing "Lang", yet could not cite to correct, instead annotated Fenggao as Hong's courtesy name—can be called rash error.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1745, line 4): Li Ciming scolds Li Xian for glossing Fenggao as the courtesy name of Hong when the Wang Gong chapter identifies Yuan Lang as Fenggao only two scrolls away.

sentences.zh: 足見當時東宮僚屬，各人分注，不相證核也。

translations.literal: Sufficient to show that at that time Eastern Palace subordinates, each person divided annotations, did not mutually verify.
translations.idiomatic: It shows how the crown prince's editorial staff annotated in silos without cross-checking.

sentences.zh: 一七四六頁九行囁不免之按：殿本考證引何焯說，謂「免」疑作「就」。

translations.literal: Page 1746 line 9: Nie did not avoid it—comment: Palace edition textual investigation cites He Zhuo, saying "avoid" suspected to be "accept".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1746, line 9): He Zhuo suggests the verb should mean "take up" the post rather than "decline" it.

sentences.zh: 集解引惠棟說，謂通鑒作「囁不之免」，胡注「不辭免也」。

translations.literal: Collected Explanations cites Hui Dong's view, saying Comprehensive Mirror writes "Nie did not it avoid", Hu annotation "did not decline".
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong adds that the Comprehensive Mirror phrases it as Xu Zhi "not declining" the appointment, which Hu Sanxing glosses as refusing to beg off.

sentences.zh: 袁宏紀作「不之起」。

translations.literal: Yuan Hong's record writes "did not it rise".
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong's chronicle, by contrast, says Xu Zhi "would not rise" to accept the office.

sentences.zh: 一七四八頁一四行躬事繼母*[繼母]*酷烈據汲本﹑殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1748 line 14: personally served stepmother *[stepmother]* fierce—according to Ji edition and Palace edition supplement.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1748, line 14): The repeated phrase "harsh stepmother" has been supplied from the Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一七四九頁二行以系嗣當立殿本考證謂「系」當作「繼」。

translations.literal: Page 1749 line 2: because heir line ought to be established—Palace edition textual investigation says "line" ought to be written "continue".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1749, line 2): The Palace edition argues for the graph meaning "heir" or "continuation."

sentences.zh: 按：集解引黃山說，謂御覽五一五引續漢書作「繼」。

translations.literal: Comment: Collected Explanations cites Huang Shan's view, saying Imperial Readings 515 citing Continued Han Book writes "continue".
translations.idiomatic: Huang Shan cites the Imperial Readings quotation of the Continued Han, which uses the "heir" graph.

sentences.zh: 系﹑系﹑繼三字古以同義通作。

translations.literal: The three graphs line, line, continue in antiquity by same meaning were interchangeably used.
translations.idiomatic: The three variant graphs were often used interchangeably in early texts.

sentences.zh: 一七五一頁六行姑執玉以告吏也按：「吏」原斗「史」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1751 line 6: mother-in-law seized Yu to report to the clerk—comment: "clerk" originally miswritten "scribe", directly corrected.
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1751, line 6): "Clerk" was misprinted as "scribe" and has been corrected.

sentences.zh: 一七五一頁一二行易曰達則兼濟天下汲本﹑殿本「濟」作「善」。

translations.literal: Page 1751 line 12: Changes says: in success then together aids the world—Ji edition and Palace edition write "aid" as "good".
translations.idiomatic: Collation (p. 1751, line 12): The Ji and Palace editions read "do good to" instead of "aid" in the quotation.

sentences.zh: 按：校補謂「窮則獨善其身，達則兼善天下」，語出孟子，注作「易曰」，誤。

translations.literal: Comment: Collation supplement says "In extremity then alone cultivates his person, in success then together does good to the world"—language comes from Mencius; commentary writing "Changes says" is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: The collation adds that the couplet comes from Mencius, not the Book of Changes, so the commentary tag is wrong.
